1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{definition}\leanok
117	[Presheaf-of-modules category in monoidal form]
118	  \label{def:monoidalPresheaf}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.MonoidalPresheaf}
120	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
121	  For a scheme \(X\), write \(\PshMod_{\otimes}(X)\) for the category of presheaves
122	  of modules over the underlying presheaf of (plain) rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\),
123	  obtained by composing the structure sheaf with the forgetful functor
124	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat} \to \mathrm{RingCat}\). This is definitionally
125	  \(X.\PshMod\), but presented in the exact form for which Mathlib equips it with
126	  the symmetric monoidal structure of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib};
127	  it is the carrier on which the objectwise tensor product of
128	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} is taken.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\begin{definition}\leanok
132	[Scheme-level module sheafification functor]
133	  \label{def:schemeModuleSheafification}
134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification}
135	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
136	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{module sheafification functor}
137	  \((-)^{\#} : X.\PshMod \to X.\Modules\) is Mathlib's sheafification functor
138	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) specialised to the identity
139	  morphism of the underlying presheaf of rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (a locally
140	  bijective map). Applying it to the objectwise tensor presheaf is what defines the
141	  sheaf tensor product \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}.
142	\end{definition}
143	
144	\begin{definition}\leanok
145	[Unit module of a scheme]
146	  \label{def:unitModule}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitModule}
148	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
149	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{unit module} \(\mathbf{1}_X \in X.\Modules\) is the
150	  structure sheaf viewed as a module over itself, i.e.\ Mathlib's
151	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.unit}\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) for
152	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It is the value of the zeroth tensor power,
153	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}).
154	\end{definition}
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
158	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
160	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
161	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
162	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
163	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
164	  % L2282-L2297).
165	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
166	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
167	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
168	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
169	  %  $$
170	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
171	  %  $$
172	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
173	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
174	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
175	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
176	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
177	  %  $$
178	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
179	  %  =
180	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
181	  %  $$"
182	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
183	  product).}
184	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
185	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
186	  \[
187	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
188	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
189	  \]
190	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
191	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
192	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
193	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
194	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
195	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
196	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
197	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
198	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
199	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
200	\end{definition}
201	
202	\begin{definition}\leanok
203	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
204	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
206	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
207	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
208	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
209	  % L4220-L4228).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
212	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
213	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
214	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
215	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
216	  %  exactly $n$ times."
217	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
218	  powers).}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
220	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
221	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
222	  on \(m\):
223	  \[
224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
225	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
226	    \qquad
227	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
228	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
229	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
230	  \]
231	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
232	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
233	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
234	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
235	  invertible.
236	\end{definition}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}\leanok
239	[Zeroth tensor power is the unit]
240	  \label{lem:tensorPow_zero}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_zero}
242	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:unitModule}
243	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
244	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\), the unit module
245	  (\cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of the recursive definition
246	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{lemma}\leanok
250	[Tensor-power recursion step]
251	  \label{lem:tensorPow_succ}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_succ}
253	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
254	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) and \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) one has
255	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
256	  \mathcal{L}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); this is the successor clause of
257	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{definition}\leanok
261	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
262	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
264	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
265	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
266	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
267	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
268	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
269	  \[
270	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
271	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
272	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
273	  \]
274	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
275	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
276	  \mathcal{F}\).
277	\end{definition}
278	
279	\begin{lemma}\leanok
280	[Zeroth twist]
281	  \label{lem:moduleTensorPow_zero}
282	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow_zero}
283	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule,
284	    def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
285	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
286	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X\)
287	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of
288	  \cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}, holding by definition, and is canonically
289	  isomorphic to \(\mathcal{F}\) via the right unitor
290	  (\cref{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}).
291	\end{lemma}
292	
293	The next four isomorphisms are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal
294	structure on \(X.\Modules\) that descend through sheafification from
295	\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} using only \emph{functoriality} of the
296	sheafification functor (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) and, for the
297	unitors, the reflective counit isomorphism --- no strong monoidality of
298	sheafification is required. They are the structural inputs of the tensor-power
299	comparison \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
300	
301	\begin{definition}\leanok
302	[Counit isomorphism of module sheafification]
303	  \label{def:sheafificationCounitIso}
304	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafificationCounitIso}
305	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
306	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) the counit of the sheafification
307	  adjunction (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
308	  \cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) gives a canonical isomorphism
309	  \((\mathcal{G})^{\#} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) of sheaves of modules,
310	  invertible because the right adjoint forgetful functor to presheaves of modules
311	  is fully faithful.
312	\end{definition}
313	
314	\begin{definition}\leanok
315	[Left unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
316	  \label{def:tensorObjUnitIso}
317	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjUnitIso}
318	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
319	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
320	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical left-unitor
321	  isomorphism \(\mathbf{1}_X \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
322	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
323	  \cref{def:unitModule}), obtained by sheafifying the presheaf left unitor of
324	  \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} and composing with the counit
325	  isomorphism \cref{def:sheafificationCounitIso}. It is the base case
326	  (\(m = 0\)) of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
327	\end{definition}
328	
329	\begin{definition}\leanok
330	[Right unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
331	  \label{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjRightUnitor}
333	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
334	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
335	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical right-unitor
336	  isomorphism \(\mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X
337	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
338	  \cref{def:unitModule}), the sheafification of the presheaf right unitor
339	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) composed with the counit
340	  isomorphism \cref{def:sheafificationCounitIso}.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{definition}\leanok
344	[Braiding of the sheaf tensor product]
345	  \label{def:tensorBraiding}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorBraiding}
347	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:schemeModuleSheafification,
348	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
349	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical
350	  braiding isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
351	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}\)
352	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), obtained by sheafifying
353	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) the symmetric braiding of the presheaf
354	  monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). It is the
355	  symmetry used in the inductive step of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
356	\end{definition}
357	
358	The strong-monoidality comparison \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
359	below is assembled from three reduction lemmas --- a localization criterion, the
360	local-isomorphism property of the unit, and their corollary --- together with the
361	coequalizer presentation of the relative module-presheaf tensor product, the single
362	Mathlib-absent brick on which the comparison rests.
363	
364	\begin{lemma}\leanok
365	[Localization criterion for module sheafification]
366	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
367	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
368	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
369	  For a morphism \(f : \mathcal{P} \to \mathcal{Q}\) of presheaves of
370	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the sheafification \((f)^{\#}\)
371	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules if
372	  and only if the underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves lies in the
373	  weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of the opens topology \(J\) on \(X\) (i.e.\ is a
374	  local isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves).
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
379	  Module sheafification (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is the localization
380	  functor at the class \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying
381	  abelian-presheaf morphism lies in \(J.W\), i.e.\ the inverse image of \(J.W\) along
382	  the forgetful functor. The localization criterion for a reflective adjunction
383	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) then gives the stated
384	  equivalence.
385	\end{proof}
386	
387	\begin{lemma}\leanok
388	[The sheafification unit is an abelian local isomorphism]
389	  \label{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
390	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
391	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
392	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the underlying
393	  abelian-group presheaf morphism of the sheafification unit
394	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\)
395	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) is the abelian sheafification
396	  unit \(\mathcal{P} \to a(\mathcal{P})\); it therefore lies in the weak-equivalence
397	  class \(J.W\) of the opens topology.
398	\end{lemma}
399	
400	\begin{proof}
401	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
402	  The forgetful functor to abelian-group presheaves carries the module
403	  sheafification unit to the unit of the abelian associated-sheaf adjunction, which
404	  is a local isomorphism, hence a member of \(J.W\).
405	\end{proof}
406	
407	\begin{lemma}\leanok
408	[Sheafification inverts the localization unit]
409	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
410	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
411	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
412	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the sheafification
413	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})^{\#}\) of the localization unit is an isomorphism of sheaves
414	  of modules. This is the un-whiskered (\(m = 0\)) special case of
415	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}.
416	\end{lemma}
417	
418	\begin{proof}
419	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
420	  Feed the abelian local-isomorphism statement
421	  \cref{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit} through the localization criterion
422	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}.
423	\end{proof}
424	
425	\begin{definition}\leanok
426	[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf]
427	  \label{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
428	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorDomainPresheaf}
429	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
430	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
431	  assignment \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\),
432	  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the underlying restriction
433	  maps, is a functor \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is
434	  the codomain row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
435	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}); functoriality follows from the
436	  functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) (identity and composition laws
437	  by \(\otimes\)-induction).
438	\end{definition}
439	
440	\begin{definition}\leanok
441	[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor triple presheaf]
442	  \label{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorTriplePresheaf}
444	  \uses{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
445	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
446	  assignment
447	  \[
448	    U \;\longmapsto\;
449	      \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
450	        \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
451	  \]
452	  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the three underlying
453	  restriction maps --- those of \(\mathcal{P}\), of the structure presheaf
454	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the middle factor restricting along the ring restriction
455	  map), and of \(\mathcal{Q}\) --- is a functor
456	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is the \emph{domain}
457	  row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
458	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}): objectwise it is exactly the
459	  triple tensor \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
460	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) on which the two
461	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps of
462	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} act, its codomain row being
463	  the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf \cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}.
464	  Functoriality (the identity and composition laws of the restriction maps)
465	  follows from the functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) --- the
466	  \(\mathrm{map\_id}\) and \(\mathrm{map\_comp}\) laws --- checked on elementary
467	  tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
468	\end{definition}
469	
470	\begin{definition}\leanok
471	[Left-action natural transformation of the coequalizer]
472	  \label{def:relTensorActL}
473	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActL}
474	  \uses{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf,
475	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
476	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
477	  \emph{left-action} natural transformation
478	  \[
479	    a_L : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
480	      (\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U))\bigr)
481	    \;\longrightarrow\;
482	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
483	  \]
484	  from the triple presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the
485	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) is, on an
486	  open \(U\), the additive map
487	  \[
488	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
489	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
490	    \longrightarrow
491	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
492	    \qquad
493	    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto (s \cdot m) \otimes n,
494	  \]
495	  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
496	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{P}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
497	  action map \(\mathrm{actLmap}\) of
498	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}; together with the parallel
499	  right-action transformation it is one of the two rows whose coequalizer
500	  presents the relative tensor presheaf \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
501	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}). Naturality in \(U\) is the
502	  compatibility of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction
503	  of \(s \cdot m\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), so \(a_L\) commutes with the
504	  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps; this is checked on
505	  elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
506	\end{definition}
507	
508	\begin{lemma}[A \(\mathbb{Z}\)-whiskered stalkwise isomorphism is a local isomorphism]
509	  \label{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}
510	  % NOTE: Lean decl name pending --- no `\lean{}` pin yet; add when the Lean
511	  % declaration for this whiskered-stalkwise-iso lemma lands.
512	  \uses{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
513	  Let \(f : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be a morphism of abelian-group
514	  presheaves on the space \(X\) that is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism} (each
515	  stalk map \(f_x\) is an isomorphism), and let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a further
516	  abelian-group presheaf. Then the whiskered map
517	  \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}\) of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor
518	  presheaves is again a stalkwise isomorphism, hence a local isomorphism (a
519	  member of \(J.W\) for the opens topology \(J\)).
520	\end{lemma}
521	
522	\begin{proof}
523	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
524	  The stalk functor \(\mathcal{A} \mapsto \mathcal{A}_x\) at a point \(x\) is a
525	  filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods of \(x\), and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\)
526	  commutes with filtered colimits, so
527	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C})_x \cong
528	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C}_x\) naturally in \(\mathcal{A}\).
529	  Under this identification the stalk of \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is
530	  \(f_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism since \(f_x\) is.
531	  Thus \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is a stalkwise isomorphism; a
532	  stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves on a space is exactly a local
533	  isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\).
534	\end{proof}

536	\begin{lemma}[Abelian universal property of the relative tensor product]
537	  \label{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
538	  \lean{TensorProduct.liftAddHom}
539	  \mathlibok
540	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Basic}).}
541	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N, P\) be \(S\)-modules (or, more
542	  generally, the abelian-group statement: \(M, N\) modules and \(P\) an additive
543	  commutative group). An additive-bilinear map
544	  \(f : M \to (N \to P)\) that is \emph{\(S\)-balanced}, i.e.\ satisfies
545	  \(f(s \cdot m)(n) = f(m)(s \cdot n)\) for all \(s \in S\), factors uniquely
546	  through the relative tensor product as an additive group homomorphism
547	  \(\widetilde{f} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with
548	  \(\widetilde{f}(m \otimes_S n) = f(m)(n)\). This is the abelian-group universal
549	  property of \(M \otimes_S N\): it is the quotient of \(M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N\)
550	  by the \(S\)-balancing relations.
551	\end{lemma}
552	
553	\begin{lemma}[The relative tensor of modules is a coequalizer of abelian groups]\leanok
554	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
555	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork,
556	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actN,
557	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actM,
558	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap,
559	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap,
560	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL,
561	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_surjective,
562	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_comp_act,
563	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aL,
564	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aR,
565	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor,
566	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_epi,
567	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition,
568	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.cofork,
569	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom,
570	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descMor,
571	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descFac,
572	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap_tmul,
573	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap_tmul,
574	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_tmul,
575	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_apply,
576	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom_tmul}
577	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
578	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N\) be \(S\)-modules. The abelian group
579	  \(M \otimes_S N\) is the coequalizer, \emph{in the category
580	  \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of abelian groups}, of the two parallel
581	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear \(S\)-action maps
582	  \[
583	    a_L,\, a_R :
584	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N)
585	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
586	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N,
587	  \]
588	  where the \emph{left} action map sends \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto
589	  (s \cdot m) \otimes n\) and the \emph{right} action map sends
590	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n)\). The coforking map is
591	  the canonical projection \(\pi : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to M \otimes_S N\),
592	  \(m \otimes n \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
593	\end{lemma}
594	
595	\begin{proof}
596	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
597	  Write \(a_N : S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to N\) and
598	  \(a_M : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} S \to M\) for the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear maps
599	  induced by the scalar action (\(s \otimes n \mapsto s \cdot n\),
600	  \(m \otimes s \mapsto s \cdot m\)); the right action map is
601	  \(a_R = \mathrm{id}_M \otimes a_N\) and the left action map is
602	  \(a_L = (a_M \otimes \mathrm{id}_N) \circ \mathrm{assoc}^{-1}\), where
603	  \(\mathrm{assoc}\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor associator. The projection
604	  \(\pi\) is the additive homomorphism produced by the abelian universal property
605	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} from the \(S\)-balanced bilinear map
606	  \((m, n) \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
607	
608	  \emph{Cofork condition.} On an elementary tensor
609	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n)\) we have
610	  \(\pi(a_L(m \otimes s \otimes n)) = (s \cdot m) \otimes_S n
611	  = m \otimes_S (s \cdot n) = \pi(a_R(m \otimes s \otimes n))\), the middle
612	  equality being the defining \(S\)-balancing relation of \(M \otimes_S N\); since
613	  elementary tensors generate, \(\pi \circ a_L = \pi \circ a_R\).
614	
615	  \emph{Universal property.} Given any abelian group \(P\) and a homomorphism
616	  \(g : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to P\) with \(g \circ a_L = g \circ a_R\), the
617	  bilinear map \((m, n) \mapsto g(m \otimes n)\) is \(S\)-balanced (that is exactly
618	  the cofork condition read on elementary tensors), so by
619	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} it factors as a unique
620	  \(\widetilde{g} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi = g\).
621	  Existence is this descended map; uniqueness follows because \(\pi\) is
622	  surjective, hence an epimorphism of abelian groups, so \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi
623	  = g\) determines \(\widetilde{g}\). Packaging existence and uniqueness gives the
624	  colimit cofork.
625	
626	\end{proof}
627	
628	\begin{lemma}[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
629	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
630	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
631	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
632	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
633	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
634	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
635	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
636	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
637	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
638	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
639	  parallel pair
640	  \[
641	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
642	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
643	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
644	  \]
645	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
646	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
647	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
648	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
649	\end{lemma}
650	
651	\begin{proof}
652	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
653	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
654	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
655	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
656	  The argument promotes the objectwise coequalizer of
657	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} to the functor category, in
658	  three steps.
659	
660	  \textbf{1. Objectwise.} For each open \(U\), apply
661	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} with \(S = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
662	  \(M = \mathcal{P}(U)\), \(N = \mathcal{Q}(U)\): the abelian group
663	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is the coequalizer
664	  in \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps
665	  \[
666	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
667	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
668	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
669	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
670	  \]
671	  with cofork map the projection \(\pi_U\).
672	
673	  \textbf{2. Naturality / promotion.} The three objectwise families assemble into
674	  morphisms of abelian-group presheaves
675	  \[
676	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
677	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
678	    \;\overset{a_L,\,a_R}{\rightrightarrows}\;
679	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
680	    \;\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}\;
681	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
682	  \]
683	  i.e.\ natural transformations of functors
684	  \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\). Naturality of
685	  \(a_L, a_R, \pi\) in \(U\) is the compatibility of the module action with the
686	  restriction maps of \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\): the restriction
687	  of \(s \cdot m\) is \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), and likewise for the projection,
688	  because the relative tensor is functorial in \(U\). Colimits in a functor
689	  category are computed objectwise --- by
690	  \cref{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib} a cocone is a colimit as
691	  soon as every evaluation \((\mathrm{ev}_U)\) of it is --- so the objectwise
692	  coequalizers of step 1 assemble into a coequalizer of the displayed parallel
693	  pair in \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\).
694	
695	  \textbf{3. Identification of the apex.} The apex presheaf
696	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the
697	  objectwise tensor formula of the presheaf monoidal structure
698	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}: \((\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p
699	  \mathcal{M}_2)(U) = \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
700	  \mathcal{M}_2(U)\)), exactly the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the
701	  relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
702	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Transporting the colimit of step 2
703	  along this identification exhibits
704	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\), as an abelian-group
705	  presheaf, as the coequalizer of the parallel pair, naturally in \(U\).
706	\end{proof}
707	
708	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise colimits in a functor category]
709	  \label{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib}
710	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Limits.evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits}
711	  \mathlibok
712	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
713	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.FunctorCategory.Basic}).}
714	  Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a category and \(\mathcal{A}\) a category in which the
715	  relevant colimits exist. A cocone over a diagram
716	  \(F : \mathcal{J} \to (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A})\) in the functor category is
717	  a colimit cocone as soon as, for every object \(U\) of \(\mathcal{D}\), its image
718	  under evaluation \(\mathrm{ev}_U : (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A}) \to \mathcal{A}\)
719	  is a colimit cocone in \(\mathcal{A}\). Equivalently, colimits in a functor
720	  category are computed objectwise.
721	\end{lemma}

723	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise value of the presheaf tensor product]
724	  \label{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
725	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_obj}
726	  \mathlibok

732	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is the objectwise relative tensor

737	  \]
738	  with the restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps.

741	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
742	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
743	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
744	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
745	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
746	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
747	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
748	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
749	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
750	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
751	  the sheafification adjunction
752	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
753	  presheaves of modules, and write
754	  \[
755	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
756	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
757	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
758	  \]
759	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
760	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
761	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
762	  \[
763	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
764	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
765	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
766	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
767	  \]
768	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
769	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
770	\end{lemma}
771	
772	\begin{proof}
773	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
774	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
775	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
776	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
777	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
778	    lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
779	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
780	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
781	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
782	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
783	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
784	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
785	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
786	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
787	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
788	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
789	
790	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
791	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
792	  is the objectwise relative tensor
793	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
794	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
795	  maps
796	  \[
797	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
798	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
799	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
800	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
801	  \]
802	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
803	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
804	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
805	  pair
806	  \[
807	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
808	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
809	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
810	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
811	  \]
812	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
813	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
814	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
815	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
816	
817	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
818	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
819	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
820	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
821	  of \(a\)-images. It remains to see that the two whiskered comparison maps
822	  relating the rows,
823	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) and
824	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
825	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), lie in \(J.W\) (equivalently, become
826	  isomorphisms after applying \(a\)).
827	
828	  \textbf{Whiskered units are stalkwise isomorphisms.} We argue this via stalks
829	  of abelian-group presheaves (\(\mathrm{TopCat.Presheaf.stalk}\), which exist
830	  for abelian presheaves on the underlying space, unlike module-sheaf stalks).
831	  The sheafification unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to a\mathcal{P}\)
832	  is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism}: for each point \(x\) the stalk map
833	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x : \mathcal{P}_x \to (a\mathcal{P})_x\) is an
834	  isomorphism (the defining property of the associated-sheaf unit). The stalk of
835	  a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of stalks,
836	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B})_x \cong
837	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B}_x\), because the stalk is a
838	  filtered colimit and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) commutes with filtered colimits.
839	  Under this identification the stalk of
840	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) at \(x\) is
841	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism
842	  (tensoring an isomorphism with the identity). Hence each whiskered unit is a
843	  stalkwise isomorphism, and a stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves
844	  on a topological space is a local isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\)
845	  (\cref{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}). Applying \(a\) therefore turns
846	  both whiskered comparison maps into isomorphisms.
847	
848	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
849	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
850	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
851	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
852	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
853	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
854	\end{proof}
855	
856	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
857	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
858	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
859	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
860	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
861	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
862	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
863	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
864	  \[
865	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
866	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
867	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
868	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
869	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
870	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
871	  \]
872	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
873	  arguments.
874	\end{corollary}
875	
876	\begin{proof}
877	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
878	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
879	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}

882	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
883	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to

886	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
887	    \qquad
888	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})

890	  \]

893	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
894	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
895	  \[

909	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }

912	  \]
913	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the

932	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
933	\end{proof}

935	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
936	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
937	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
938	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
939	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
940	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
941	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
942	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
943	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
944	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
945	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
946	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
947	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
948	  %  (formulation omitted)."
949	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
950	  power isomorphisms).}
951	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
952	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
953	  \[
954	    \mu_{m,m'} :

956	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
957	  \]

959	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
960	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
961	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
962	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
963	\end{lemma}
964	
965	\begin{proof}
966	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
967	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
968	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
969	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
970	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
971	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
972	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
973	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
974	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
975	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
976	
977	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
978	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
979	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
980	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
981	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
982	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
983	  sheaf tensor products
984	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
985	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
986	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
987	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
988	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
989	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
990	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
991	  of the tensor product is never used.
992	
993	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
994	  \(m = 0\),
995	  \[
996	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
997	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}

999	  \]

1009	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})

1014	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
5	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{The affine case}
70	
71	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}
72	
73	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
74	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
75	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
76	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
77	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
78	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
79	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
80	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
81	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
82	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
83	
84	\begin{lemma}\leanok
85	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
86	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
89	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
90	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
91	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
92	\end{lemma}
93	
94	\begin{proof}
95	
96	
97	
98	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
99	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
100	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
101	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
102	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
103	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
104	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
105	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
106	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
107	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
108	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
109	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
110	\end{proof}
111	
112	\begin{lemma}\leanok
113	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
114	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
115	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
116	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
117	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
118	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
119	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
120	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
121	  isomorphism.
122	\end{lemma}
123	
124	\begin{proof}
125	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
126	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
127	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
128	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
129	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
130	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
131	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
132	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
133	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
134	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
135	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}\leanok
139	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
140	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
142	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
143	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
144	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
145	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
146	\end{lemma}
147	
148	\begin{proof}
149	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
150	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
151	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
152	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
153	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
154	  affine opens.
155	\end{proof}
156	
157	\subsection{The affine computation}
158	
159	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
160	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
161	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
162	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
163	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
164	
165	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
166	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
167	their proof sketches.
168	
169	\begin{lemma}
170	
171	\leanok
172	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
173	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
176	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
178	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
179	  \[
180	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
181	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
182	  \]
183	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
184	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
185	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{proof}
189	
190	
191	
192	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
193	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
194	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
195	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
196	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
197	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
198	\end{proof}
199	
200	\begin{lemma}
201	
202	\leanok
203	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
204	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
206	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
207	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
208	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
209	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
210	  \[
211	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
212	    \;\cong\;
213	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
214	  \]
215	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
216	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
217	  of \(N\).
218	\end{lemma}
219	
220	\begin{proof}
221	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
222	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
223	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
224	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
225	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
226	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
227	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
228	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
229	  equation of
230	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
231	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
232	\end{proof}
233	
234	\begin{lemma}
235	
236	\leanok
237	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
238	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
240	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
241	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
242	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
243	  \[
244	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
245	    \;\cong\;
246	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
247	  \]
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
252	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
253	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
254	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
255	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
256	\end{proof}
257	
258	\begin{lemma}
259	
260	\leanok
261	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
262	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
264	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
265	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
266	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
267	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
268	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
269	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
270	  \[
271	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
272	    \;\cong\;
273	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
274	  \]
275	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
276	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
277	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of these isomorphisms is moreover
278	  natural in the open argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two
279	  sides commute with the isomorphisms, so the family is a natural isomorphism of the
280	  two presheaves of \(R\)-modules in the open variable (this naturality is
281	  established in the proof below). For \(U = D(a)\) it is exactly the linear
282	  equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement~(1) in the proof of
283	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
284	\end{lemma}
285	
286	\begin{proof}
287	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
288	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
289	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
290	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
291	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
292	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
293	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
294	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
295	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
296	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
297	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
298	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
299	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with
300	  further restriction along an inclusion of opens.
301	
302	  \medskip
303	  \emph{Naturality of the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) in the open (remark).} The family is
304	  in fact a natural isomorphism between two presheaves of \(R\)-modules on
305	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\): the source presheaf
306	  \(U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\), with the
307	  structure-sheaf restriction maps of the pushforward, and the target presheaf
308	  \(U \mapsto
309	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
310	  with the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\) transported through the preimage
311	  operation \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and read as \(R\)-linear maps by
312	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Concretely, for any inclusion of opens
313	  \(U' \subseteq U\), with \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
314	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square with horizontal maps
315	  \(e_U, e_{U'}\) and vertical maps the respective structure-sheaf restrictions
316	  commutes. The reason is structural: each \(e_U\) is a composite whose constituents
317	  are of exactly two kinds — structure-sheaf restriction maps (of \(N\),
318	  respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)) and identity-on-carrier
319	  \(\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) repackagings (conjugation by the \emph{fixed},
320	  open-independent ring map supplied by the restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and
321	  by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
322	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}). Restriction maps commute
323	  with further restriction by the presheaf functoriality axiom (using
324	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
325	  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\), as \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) is
326	  monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because the
327	  ring map does not depend on \(U\); the naturality square is the paste of these
328	  component squares, and each \(e_U\) being an isomorphism makes the family a natural
329	  isomorphism. This open-naturality is exactly the compatibility invoked, at the
330	  inclusion \(D(a) \subseteq \top\), in the proof of
331	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
332	\end{proof}
333	
334	\begin{lemma}
335	
336	\leanok
337	[The tilde restriction from the top open to a basic open is a localization]
338	  \label{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
340	  Let \(M\) be an \(R'\)-module and \(b \in R'\). Then the structure-sheaf
341	  restriction map
342	  \[
343	    \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(b)),
344	  \]
345	  read as a map of \(R'\)-modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) as the
346	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). This is
347	  the \(R'\)-side localization fact transported in movement~(3) of the proof of
348	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
349	\end{lemma}
350	
351	\begin{proof}
352	
353	
354	  By the defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), the tilde-localization map
355	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) exhibits its target as the localization of
356	  \(M\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). The global-sections map
357	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) is the localization of \(M\) at the trivial
358	  submonoid \(\mathrm{powers}(1)\) — since \(D(1) = \top\) — and is therefore
359	  bijective. The triangle identity relating these two localization maps to the
360	  structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
361	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) then exhibits the restriction itself, post-composed
362	  with the inverse of the bijection \(M \cong \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\), as the
363	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\).
364	\end{proof}
365	
366	We record three further bespoke supports that drive the basic-open verification of
367	the affine-pushforward isomorphism below. They carry no external citation and stand
368	on their proof sketches.
369	
370	\begin{lemma}
371	
372	\leanok
373	[Restriction of scalars of a localized module]
374	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}
375	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
376	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
377	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
378	  \(M\) at the image submonoid
379	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S) \subseteq A\). Then the underlying \(R\)-linear map
380	  \(\operatorname{restr}_R f\) exhibits \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\)
381	  itself.
382	\end{lemma}
383	
384	\begin{proof}
385	
386	
387	
388	  This is the exact converse of the standard fact that a localization of \(M\) at a
389	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
390	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
391	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each
392	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(N\) because its image
393	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) does and the two actions coincide by the scalar
394	  tower \(R \to A \to N\). Surjectivity of \(f\) up to denominators and the
395	  equality-witness condition transport along the map
396	  \(s \mapsto \operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) from \(S\) onto
397	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\), again using
398	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\cdot{} = s\cdot{}\). In the application
399	  \(A = R'\), \(S = \mathrm{powers}(a)\), one has
400	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\), so
401	  this identifies the localization of \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at \(a\)
402	  with the localization of \(M\) at \(\varphi a\); it is the ring-change core of the
403	  basic-open computation below.
404	\end{proof}
405	
406	\begin{lemma}
407	
408	\leanok
409	[Section-level counit isomorphism from a localization hypothesis]
410	  \label{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
411	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule}
412	  Let \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-module and \(a \in R\). Suppose the
413	  structure-sheaf restriction map \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\), read as
414	  an \(R\)-linear map of the global-section modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as
415	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then the
416	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
417	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N : \widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)} \to N\) restricts to an
418	  isomorphism on the sections over the basic open \(D(a)\).
419	\end{lemma}
420	
421	\begin{proof}
422	
423	
424	
425	  Over \(D(a)\) the section map \(L\) of the counit sits in the triangle identity
426	  \(L \circ j = \rho\), where
427	  \(j : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma\bigl(\widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)}, D(a)\bigr)\) is
428	  the tilde-localization map and \(\rho : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) is
429	  the structure-sheaf restriction. Both \(j\) and \(\rho\) exhibit their targets as
430	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N,\top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) — \(j\) by the
431	  defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), and \(\rho\) by hypothesis. By the
432	  uniqueness of localized modules there is a unique \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\)
433	  with \(e \circ j = \rho\); the triangle identity then forces \(L = e\), so \(L\) is
434	  an isomorphism. (This is the per-basic-open input feeding the basis-local
435	  criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}.)
436	\end{proof}
437	
438	\begin{lemma}
439	
440	\leanok
441	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module, conditional form]
442	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional}
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule}
444	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
445	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
446	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and \(M\) an
447	  \(R'\)-module. Suppose that for every \(a \in R\) the structure-sheaf restriction
448	  of the pushforward \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) exhibits its
449	  sections over \(D(a)\) as the localization of its global sections at
450	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
451	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
452	  \[
453	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M \;\cong\;
454	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)} .
455	  \]
456	\end{lemma}
457	
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
460	  Write \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\). Under the hypothesis,
461	  Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized} applies for each \(a \in R\),
462	  so the counit \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) restricts to an isomorphism on the
463	  sections over every basic open \(D(a)\). Since the basic opens form a basis of
464	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), the basis-local criterion
465	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} upgrades this to: the counit
466	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) is an isomorphism, i.e.\ \(N\) lies in the
467	  essential image of \(\widetilde{(-)}\). Composing the inverse counit with the
468	  tilde of the global-sections comparison
469	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} yields the asserted object isomorphism.
470	\end{proof}
471	
472	\begin{lemma}\leanok
473	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
474	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
476	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
477	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
478	        lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt,
479	        lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
480	% NOTE: the carrier-wall obstruction to `hloc` is resolved (restriction-of-scalars /
481	% scalar-tower instances feed lem:powers_restrictScalars; lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule
482	% supplies the R'-side localization). The single remaining obligation is the open-naturality of
483	% the section comparison e_{D(a)}, established as a prose remark in the proof of
484	% lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt; the proof sketch below derives each per-a hloc(a) from the top-level
485	% localization plus that naturality square for the inclusion D(a) ⊆ ⊤, so no section-level square
486	% is hand-proved per a.
487	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
488	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
489	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
490	  \[
491	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
492	    \;\cong\;
493	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
494	  \]
495	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
496	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
497	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
498	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
499	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
500	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
