945	  with the component at the free sheaf \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) of the pullback-identity
946	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
947	\end{lemma}
948	
949	\begin{proof}
950	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
951	  Both sides are cocones on the coproduct \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\); by coproduct
952	  extensionality it suffices to check equality after precomposing with each coprojection
953	  \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element case
954	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}.
955	\end{proof}
956	
957	\begin{lemma}\leanok
958	[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
959	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
960	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
961	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
962	  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
963	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
964	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
965	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
966	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).
967	\end{lemma}
968	
969	\begin{proof}
970	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp,
971	    lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
972	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
973	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
974	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
975	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The compatibility of the mate
976	  with the adjunction hom-equivalences is supplied by
977	  \cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}, the reusable conjugate-naturality engine. The
978	  pentagon coherence of the iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation
979	  well-defined.
980	\end{proof}
981	
982	\begin{lemma}\leanok
983	[Mate/conjugate compatibility of the adjunction hom-equivalence]
984	  \label{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
985	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
986	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2\) be two adjunctions and \(\alpha\) a natural
987	  transformation between their left adjoints. For a morphism \(f\) the hom-equivalence of
988	  \(\mathrm{adj}_2\) applied to \(\alpha_c \circ f\) equals the hom-equivalence of
989	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1\) applied to \(f\), post-composed with the component at \(d\) of the
990	  conjugate (mate) of \(\alpha\). This is the general naturality of the conjugate
991	  construction against the two hom-equivalences; it is the reusable engine behind
992	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
993	\end{lemma}
994	
995	\begin{proof}
996	  Both sides unfold to the same pasting of unit/counit triangles for the two adjunctions;
997	  the equality is the standard compatibility of the mate of \(\alpha\) with the
998	  hom-set bijections.
999	\end{proof}
1000	
1001	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1002	[Composite coherence at the free sheaf]
1003	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1004	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1005	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1006	  The composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}: for composable \(a, b\) and an
1007	  index set \(I\), the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b \circ a\) at \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\)
1008	  factors through \(a^{*}\) of the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b\), the one for \(a\), and
1009	  the iterated-pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}.
1010	\end{lemma}
1011	
1012	\begin{proof}
1013	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1014	  By coproduct extensionality it suffices to check the identity after precomposing with each
1015	  coprojection \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element
1016	  composite coherence \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} (with the base-ring sheaf
1017	  tracked through each pullback).
1018	\end{proof}
1019	
1020	Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1021	upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
1022	identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
1023	composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
1024	\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
1025	functoriality laws (identity and composition) of the Grassmannian
1026	functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.
1027	
1028	\begin{definition}
1029	
1030	\leanok
1031	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
1032	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
1033	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
1034	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
1035	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1036	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1037	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1038	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
1039	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1040	  % "represents the
1041	  %  contravariant functor
1042	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1043	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1044	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1045	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1046	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
1047	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1048	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
1049	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
1050	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
1051	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
1052	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
1053	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
1054	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
1055	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1056	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
1057	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
1058	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
1059	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
1060	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
1061	  \[
1062	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
1063	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
1064	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
1065	  \]
1066	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
1067	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
1068	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
1069	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
1070	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
1071	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
1072	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
1073	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
1074	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
1075	\end{definition}
1076	
1077	\section{The universal property}
1078	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
1079	
1080	\begin{theorem}
1081	
1082	\leanok
1083	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
1084	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
1085	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
1086	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
1087	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
1088	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1089	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1090	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1091	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
1092	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1093	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
1094	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
1095	  %  contravariant functor
1096	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1097	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1098	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1099	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1100	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
1101	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
1102	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
1103	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1104	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
1105	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
1106	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
1107	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
1108	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
1109	  \[
1110	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
1111	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1112	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
1113	    \qquad
1114	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
1115	      \;\longmapsto\;
1116	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
1117	  \]
1118	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
1119	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1120	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
1121	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
1122	  universal family.
1123	\end{theorem}
1124	
1125	\begin{proof}
1126	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
1127	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
1128	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
1129	    def:grassmannian_functor}
1130	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
1131	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
1132	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
1133	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
1134	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
1135	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
1136	
1137	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
1138	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
1139	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
1140	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
1141	  the composite
1142	  \[
1143	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
1144	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1145	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
1146	  \]
1147	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
1148	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
1149	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
1150	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
1151	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
1152	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
1153	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
1154	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
1155	
1156	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
1157	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
1158	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
1159	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
1160	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
1161	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
1162	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
1163	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
1164	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
1165	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
1166	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
1167	
1168	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
1169	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
1170	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
1171	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
1172	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
1173	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
1174	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
1175	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
1176	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
1177	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
1178	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
1179	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
1180	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
1181	
1182	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
1183	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
1184	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
1185	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
1186	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
1187	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
1188	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
1189	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
1190	
1191	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
1192	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
1193	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1194	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1195	\end{proof}

1029	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
1030	
1031	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
1032	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
1033	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
1034	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
1035	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
1036	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
1037	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
1038	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
1039	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1040	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
1041	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
1042	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
1043	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
1044	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
1045	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
1046	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
1047	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
1048	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
1049	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
1050	\end{proof}

1052	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
1053	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
1054	
1055	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
1056	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
1057	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
1058	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
1059	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
1060	global sections.
1061	
1062	\begin{definition}\leanok
1063	[Section multiplication]
1064	  \label{def:sectionMul}
1065	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
1066	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1067	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1068	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
1069	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
1070	  \[
1071	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
1072	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
1073	    \;\longrightarrow\;
1074	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
1075	  \]
1076	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
1077	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
1078	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
1079	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
1080	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
1081	  the sheafification unit
1082	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
1083	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
1084	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
1085	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
1086	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
1087	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
1088	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
1089	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
1090	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
1091	\end{definition}
1092	
1093	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
1094	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
1095	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
1096	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1097	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
1098	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
1099	  the two composites
1100	  \[
1101	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
1102	    \longrightarrow
1103	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
1104	    \cong
1105	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
1106	  \]
1107	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
1108	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
1109	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
1110	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
1111	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
1112	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
1113	\end{lemma}
1114	
1115	\begin{proof}
1116	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1117	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1118	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
1119	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
1120	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
1121	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
1122	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
1123	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
1124	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
1125	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
1126	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
1127	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
1128	  section multiplications.
1129	\end{proof}

1131	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
1132	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
1133	
1134	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
1135	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
1136	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
1137	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
1138	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
1139	
1140	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
1141	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1142	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
1143	  \mathlibok
1144	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
1145	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1146	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
1147	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
1148	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
1149	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
1150	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
1151	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
1152	  \(1 \in A_0\).
1153	\end{lemma}
1154	
1155	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
1156	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
1157	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
1158	  \mathlibok
1159	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
1160	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
1161	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
1162	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1163	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
1164	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
1165	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
1166	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
1167	  extension of the graded action.
1168	\end{lemma}

1170	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
1171	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1172	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1173	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
1174	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
1175	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
1176	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
1177	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
1178	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
1179	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1180	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
1181	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
1182	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
1183	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
1184	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
1185	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
1186	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
1187	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
1188	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
1189	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
1190	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
1191	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
1192	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
1193	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
1194	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
1195	  multiplication is the composite
1196	  \[
1197	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
1198	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
1199	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
1200	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
1201	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
1202	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
1203	  \]
1204	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
1205	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
1206	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
1207	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
1208	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
1209	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
1210	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
1211	\end{lemma}

1213	\begin{proof}
1214	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
1215	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1216	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
1217	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
1218	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of

1220	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined

1222	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
1223	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
1224	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}

922	
923	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
924	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
925	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
926	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
927	
928	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
929	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
930	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
931	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
932	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
933	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
934	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
935	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
936	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
937	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
938	
939	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
940	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
941	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
942	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
943	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
944	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
945	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
946	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
947	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
948	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
949	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
950	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
951	
952	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
953	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
954	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
955	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
956	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
957	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
958	
959	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
960	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
961	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
962	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
963	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
964	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
965	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
966	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
967	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
968	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
969	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
970	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
971	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
972	
973	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
974	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
975	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
976	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
977	\end{proof}
978	
979	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
980	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
981	
982	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
983	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
984	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
985	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
986	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
987	
988	\begin{lemma}\leanok
989	[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
990	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
991	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
992	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
993	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
994	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
995	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
996	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
997	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
998	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
999	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1000	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1001	\end{lemma}
1002	\begin{proof}
1003	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1004	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1005	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1006	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1007	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1008	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1009	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1010	\end{proof}
1011	
1012	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1013	[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1014	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1015	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1016	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1017	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1018	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1019	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1020	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1021	\end{lemma}
1022	\begin{proof}
1023	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1024	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1025	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1026	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1027	\end{proof}
1028	
1029	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1030	[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1031	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1032	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1033	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1034	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1035	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1036	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1037	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1038	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1039	\end{lemma}
1040	\begin{proof}
1041	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1042	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1043	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1044	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1045	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1046	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1047	  these conditions gives the claim.
1048	\end{proof}
1049	
1050	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1051	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1052	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1053	  \mathlibok
1054	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1055	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1056	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1057	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1058	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1059	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1060	\end{lemma}

1062	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1063	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1064	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1065	  \mathlibok
1066	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1067	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1068	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1069	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1070	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).
1071	\end{lemma}
1072	
1073	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1074	[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1075	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1076	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1077	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1078	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1079	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1080	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1081	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1082	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1083	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1084	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1085	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1086	\end{lemma}
1087	
1088	\begin{proof}
1089	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1090	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1091	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1092	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1093	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1094	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1095	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1096	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1097	
1098	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1099	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1100	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1101	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1102	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1103	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1104	
1105	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1106	  \[
1107	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1108	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1109	  \]
1110	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1111	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1112	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1113	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1114	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1115	
1116	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1117	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1118	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1119	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1120	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1121	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1122	\end{proof}
1123	
1124	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1125	[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1126	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1127	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1128	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1129	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1130	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1131	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1132	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1133	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1134	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1135	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1136	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1137	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1138	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1139	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1140	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1141	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1142	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1143	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1144	  core of the induction).}
1145	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1146	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1147	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1148	
1149	\end{lemma}
1150	
1151	\begin{proof}
1152	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1153	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1154	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1155	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1156	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1157	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1158	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1159	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1160	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1161	
1162	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1163	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1164	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1165	
1166	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1167	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1168	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1169	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1170	  \begin{enumerate}
1171	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1172	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1173	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1174	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1175	      \(P_d\)-modules
1176	      \[
1177	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1178	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1179	      \]
1180	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1181	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1182	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1183	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1184	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1185	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1186	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1187	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1188	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1189	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1190	      to \(A_g\).
1191	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1192	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1193	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1194	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1195	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1196	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1197	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1198	      over \(A_f\).
1199	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1200	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1201	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1202	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1203	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1204	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1205	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1206	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1207	      completes the induction.
1208	  \end{enumerate}
1209	\end{proof}

1255	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1256	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1257	
1258	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1259	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1260	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1261	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1262	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1263	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1264	restricted sheaf over the base open.
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
1267	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1268	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
1269	  \mathlibok
1270	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
1271	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
1272	  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
1273	  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
1274	  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
1275	\end{lemma}
1276	
1277	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1278	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1279	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1281	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1282	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1283	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1284	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1285	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1286	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1287	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1288	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1289	\end{lemma}
1290	
1291	\begin{proof}
1292	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1293	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1294	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1295	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1296	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1297	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1298	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1299	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1300	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1301	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1302	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1303	\end{proof}

1305	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1306	[Half 1 — G1 locality reduction over a finite basic-open cover]
1307	  \label{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1308	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1309	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1310	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1311	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof, gluing step
1312	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2107-L2109).
1313	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1314	  % "Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$. Hence we conclude by
1315	  %  Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover}."
1316	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (gluing step,
1317	  Algebra Lemma \texttt{lemma-cover}).}
1318	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(X\), and
1319	  \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) is a finite
1320	  family with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) (so the basic opens \(D(g)\),
1321	  \(g \in t\), cover \(W\)), and that for each \(g \in t\) the section module
1322	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). Then \(\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1323	  is a finite \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1324	\end{lemma}
1325	
1326	\begin{proof}
1327	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1328	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1329	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (gluing step).}
1330	  By the local-on-a-spanning-family criterion \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1331	  it suffices to show, for each \(g \in t\), that the localization of
1332	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\) is finite over the localization
1333	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). Fix \(g \in t\). The basic open \(D(g)\) is affine, and the
1334	  restriction \(\Gamma(X, W) \to \Gamma(X, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) as
1335	  the away-localization \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1336	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); moreover the section restriction
1337	  \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the
1338	  localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1339	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). Thus \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is a
1340	  model of the localized module, finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = (\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1341	  by hypothesis; the model-transfer lemma
1342	  \cref{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer} carries this finiteness to the
1343	  canonical localized module of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at the powers of \(g\), over
1344	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). This supplies the per-element hypothesis, and
1345	  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan} concludes that \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is
1346	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1347	\end{proof}

1349	\subsection{Transport engine for generating sections}
1350	
1351	The restriction-of-generators lemma below is assembled from a generic transport
1352	principle: a finite generating family of a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) pushes
1353	forward to a finite generating family of \(F(\mathcal{M})\) along any functor \(F\) of
1354	sheaves of modules that preserves colimits and carries the structure sheaf to the
1355	structure sheaf. The three blocks of this subsection package that principle (the
1356	generating family, its index type, and the finiteness bookkeeping). They are
1357	project-bespoke: Mathlib provides the analogous transport only for full presentations
1358	(with kernel data), not for bare generating families.
1359	
1360	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1361	[Transport of a generating family along a colimit-preserving functor]
1362	  \label{lem:genSections_map}
1363	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map}
1364	  Let \(F : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to \mathrm{Mod}\,S\) be a functor of sheaves of modules
1365	  that preserves colimits and is equipped with an isomorphism
1366	  \(\eta : F(\OO_R) \xrightarrow{\sim} \OO_S\) carrying the unit (structure sheaf) of the
1367	  source to that of the target. Given a generating family \(\sigma\) of \(\mathcal{M}\)
1368	  with index set \(I = \sigma.I\) and epimorphism
1369	  \(\sigma.\pi : \OO_R^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{M}\), the images
1370	  \((\mathrm{mapFree}\,F\,\eta\,I)^{-1} \circ F(\sigma.\pi)\) form a generating family of
1371	  \(F(\mathcal{M})\) with the same index set \(I\). The map is an epimorphism because it
1372	  is the composite of an isomorphism with \(F(\sigma.\pi)\), and \(F\) preserves the
1373	  epimorphism \(\sigma.\pi\).
1374	\end{lemma}
1375	
1376	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1377	[The transported family keeps its index type]
1378	  \label{lem:genSections_map_I}
1379	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_I}
1380	  \uses{lem:genSections_map}
1381	  The index set of the transported generating family of \cref{lem:genSections_map} equals
1382	  the index set of the original: \((\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta)\!.I = \sigma.I\). This
1383	  holds definitionally.
1384	\end{lemma}
1385	
1386	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1387	[Finiteness survives transport]
1388	  \label{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1389	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_isFiniteType}
1390	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I}
1391	  If the original generating family \(\sigma\) is of finite type (its index set is finite),
1392	  then so is the transported family \(\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta\): the index type is
1393	  unchanged by \cref{lem:genSections_map_I}, hence still finite.
1394	\end{lemma}
1395	
1396	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1397	[Half 2, seam 1a — local generators restrict to a smaller open]
1398	  \label{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1399	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1400	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1401	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
1402	  Suppose that on an open \(Y \subseteq X\) the restriction \(\F|_Y\) is
1403	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Then for any open \(V \le Y\) the
1404	  restricted family — the images of those generators under the section-restriction
1405	  \(\Gamma(\F, Y) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) — is again a finite generating family of
1406	  \(\F|_V\). Generation survives passage to the smaller open (a family that generates
1407	  every stalk over \(Y\) generates every stalk over \(V\)), and the index set, and hence
1408	  finiteness, is unchanged.
1409	\end{lemma}
1410	
1411	\begin{proof}
1412	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1413	  A family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_Y\) precisely when the
1414	  induced map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) is surjective on every stalk
1415	  \(\F_y\,(y \in Y)\). Restricting along the inclusion \(V \le Y\) the
1416	  generators map to \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\), and for a point \(x \in V\) the stalk
1417	  \(\F_x\) and the comparison map agree with the value at \(x\) computed over \(Y\), since
1418	  stalks are insensitive to shrinking the ambient open. Hence the induced map
1419	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \to \F|_V\) is surjective on every stalk over \(V\), so
1420	  \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_V\). The index set \(I\) is the same
1421	  finite set, so finiteness is preserved. Concretely the restricted family is obtained by
1422	  transporting the generating family along the (colimit-preserving, unit-preserving)
1423	  restriction functors via the transport engine \cref{lem:genSections_map}, whose index
1424	  type is unchanged (\cref{lem:genSections_map_I}) and which preserves finiteness
1425	  (\cref{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}).
1426	\end{proof}
1427	
1428	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1429	[Half 2, seam 1b — finite standard-basic-open subcover of an affine open]
1430	  \label{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1431	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1432	  Let \(W\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), with the canonical identification
1433	  \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\). Given an arbitrary open cover \(\{U_\alpha\}\) of \(W\),
1434	  there is a finite family \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1435	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that each basic open \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\)) is
1436	  contained in some cover member \(U_\alpha\). In particular the \(D(g)\,(g \in t)\) form a
1437	  finite standard-basic-open subcover of \(W\) refining \(\{U_\alpha\}\).
1438	\end{lemma}
1439	
1440	\begin{proof}
1441	  Under \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) the basic opens \(D(g)\,(g \in \Gamma(X, W))\) form a
1442	  basis of the topology (\(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}\)). Hence each
1443	  cover member \(U_\alpha\) is a union of basic opens, and the collection of all basic
1444	  opens \(D(g)\) that are contained in some \(U_\alpha\) still covers \(W\). The affine
1445	  scheme \(W\) is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens
1446	  \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already cover \(W\); set
1447	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\), a finite family, each \(D(g_i)\)
1448	  contained in some \(U_\alpha\) by construction. That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1449	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) — i.e. no prime ideal contains all the \(g_i\) — is exactly
1450	  the condition \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\).
1451	\end{proof}
1452	
1453	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1454	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1456	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a sheaf of modules on \(Y\). Then
1457	  \(\F\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1458	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1459	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\). No quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is assumed:
1460	  the statement holds for arbitrary sheaves of modules on \(Y\), and applies in particular
1461	  to the restrictions \(\F|_{D(g)}\) arising in the cover assembly.
1462	\end{lemma}
1463	
1464	\begin{proof}
1465	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1466	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1467	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1468	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1469	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1470	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1471	  finite generating family.
1472	\end{proof}
1473

1156	\subsection*{The away-localisation pullback isomorphism}
1157	
1158	\begin{definition}
1159	
1160	\leanok
1161	[Pullback of two principal opens of \(\Spec A\)]
1162	  \label{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}
1163	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayPullbackIso}
1164	  \uses{lem:mathlib_pullbackSpecIso, lem:mathlib_isLocalization_away_mul, lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}
1165	  Let \(A\) be a commutative ring and \(x, y \in A\). The fibre product of the two
1166	  basic-open inclusions \(\Spec A[1/x] \to \Spec A \leftarrow \Spec A[1/y]\) is
1167	  canonically isomorphic to the spectrum of the doubly-inverted localisation:
1168	  \[
1169	    \Spec A[1/x] \times_{\Spec A} \Spec A[1/y]
1170	      \;\cong\; \Spec A[1/(xy)].
1171	  \]

1178	  ring \(A\) so that it may be instantiated at the triple-overlap rings of the
1179	  Grassmannian glue datum.

1307	\subsection*{The triple-overlap glue field \(t'\)}
1308	
1309	The scheme-gluing machine requires, beyond the pairwise transitions \(t_{I,J}\), a
1310	\emph{triple-overlap} field \(t'\) on the relevant fibre products, together with
1311	its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) with the chart projections. We record both
1312	as the next layer of bridge results; their cocycle coherence is the residual
1313	obligation discharged in \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}.
1314	
1315	\begin{definition}
1316	
1317	\leanok
1318	[The triple-overlap transition \(t'\)]
1319	  \label{def:gr_chart_transition'}
1320	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'}
1321	  \uses{def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}
1322	  The \(t'\)-field of the Grassmannian glue datum is the morphism of schemes
1323	  \[
1324	    t'_{I,J,K} : U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K \longrightarrow U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I ,
1325	  \]
1326	  defined as the composite
1327	  \[
1328	    U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K
1329	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Spec S_I
1330	      \xrightarrow{\ \Spec \Theta_{I,J}\ } \Spec S_J
1331	      \xrightarrow{\ \Spec \mathrm{swap}\ } \Spec R^J[1/(P^J_K P^J_I)]
1332	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I ,
1333	  \]
1334	  where the first and last arrows are the pullback identifications of
1335	  \cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso} -- the source one for the pair \((P^I_J, P^I_K)\)
1336	  over \(R^I\), and the target one (taken inverse) for the pair \((P^J_K, P^J_I)\)
1337	  over \(R^J\) -- the middle arrow is the \(\Spec\) of the localised
1338	  triple-overlap transition \(\Theta_{I,J} : S_J \to S_I\)
1339	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}), and \(\mathrm{swap}\) is the \(\Spec\) of the
1340	  order-swap isomorphism \(R^J[1/(P^J_K P^J_I)] \cong S_J = R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)]\)
1341	  (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}). The order-swap is inserted precisely to
1342	  reconcile the product order \(P^J_K P^J_I\) carried by the target pullback
1343	  identification with the order \(P^J_I P^J_K\) of the codomain \(S_J\) of
1344	  \(\Theta_{I,J}\).
1345	\end{definition}
1346	
1347	\begin{lemma}
1348	
1349	\leanok
1350	[The \(t'\) ring identity]
1351	  \label{lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity}
1352	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'_ringIdentity}
1353	  \uses{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap, lem:mathlib_away_lift}
1354	  Over the triple-overlap rings, the localised transition \(\Theta_{I,J}\)
1355	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) pre-composed with the order-swap and the right
1356	  inclusion equals the left inclusion post-composed with the plain transition
1357	  \(\theta_{I,J}\), as ring homomorphisms \(R^J[1/P^J_I] \to S_I\):
1358	  \[
1359	    \Theta_{I,J} \circ \mathrm{swap} \circ \iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{P^J_K, P^J_I}
1360	      \;=\; \iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{P^I_J, P^I_K} \circ \theta_{I,J} .
1361	  \]
1362	\end{lemma}

1364	  \uses{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap, lem:mathlib_away_lift}
1365	  \leanok

1367	  universal property of the localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) it suffices
1368	  to check equality after pre-composing with the structure map
1369	  \(R^J \to R^J[1/P^J_I]\), i.e.\ on the chart ring \(R^J\). There the right
1370	  inclusion and the order-swap collapse to structure maps
1371	  (\cref{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap},
1372	  \cref{lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap}), and the lift defining
1373	  \(\Theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) unwinds to
1374	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}} \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
1375	  (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}); the right side likewise unwinds through the lift
1376	  defining \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) to the same
1377	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}} \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}\). Proved directly in Lean.

1380	\begin{lemma}
1381

1411	\begin{lemma}
1412	
1413	\leanok
1414	[The triple-overlap cocycle coherence]
1415	  \label{lem:gr_chartTransition'_cocycle}
1416	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'_cocycle}
1417	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition', lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id, def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}
1418	  The triple-overlap transitions \(t'\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}) satisfy
1419	  the cocycle coherence on the source triple overlap \(U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\):
1420	  \[
1421	    t'_{I,J,K} \;\circ\; t'_{J,K,I} \;\circ\; t'_{K,I,J} \;=\; \mathrm{id}.
1422	  \]
1423	  This is the \(\mathrm{cocycle}\) field of the Grassmannian glue datum -- the
1424	  categorical realisation of the rotated ring identity \(\Phi = \id\)
1425	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id}).
1426	\end{lemma}
1427	\begin{proof}
1428	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition', lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id, def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}
1429	  \leanok
1430	  Expand each factor \(t'\) into its constituents
1431	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}): a source pullback identification, the
1432	  \(\Spec\) of a localised triple-overlap transition \(\Theta\)
1433	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) pre-composed with the \(\Spec\) of an
1434	  order-swap (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}), and an inverse target pullback
1435	  identification. In the threefold composite the two internal pairs
1436	  \(\mathrm{ap}^{-1} \circ \mathrm{ap}\) of pullback identifications
1437	  (\cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}) cancel, adjacent factors sharing the same
1438	  identification. There remain the six \(\Spec\)-arrows of the three \(\Theta\)'s
1439	  and three order-swaps; these collapse into a single \(\Spec\) of their composite
1440	  ring endomorphism \(\Phi\). By \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id} one has
1441	  \(\Phi = \id\), so this middle \(\Spec\) is the identity, and the outer
1442	  conjugating pullback isomorphism cancels against its inverse, leaving the
1443	  identity of the source triple overlap. Proved directly in Lean.
1444	\end{proof}

1524	\begin{lemma}[Affine $\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma$ adjunction with its $\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ counit]
1525	  \label{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib}

1529	  On the affine scheme \(\Spec R\) the associated-sheaf functor
1530	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules}\) is left adjoint to the
1531	  affine section functor \(\Gamma : (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules} \to \mathrm{Mod}\,R\) (Mathlib's
1532	  \(\mathrm{moduleSpecΓFunctor}\), evaluation of a sheaf of modules at the top open). The counit of
1533	  this adjunction is the natural transformation
1534	  \(\Gamma(-)\,\widetilde{\phantom{x}} \to \mathrm{id}\) whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\)
1535	  is \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \top)} \to \mathcal{M}\), and
1536	  the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful.

1549	  % epi over an affine.
1550	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1551	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
1552	  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
1553	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
1554	  generated sheaf over an affine).}
1555	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
1556	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1557	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
1558	  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
1559	  \(B\)-module map.
1560	\end{lemma}
1561	
1562	\begin{proof}
1563	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1564	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1565	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1566	  affine).}
1567	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1568	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1569	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1570	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1571	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1572	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1573	
1574	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1575	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1576	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1577	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1578	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1579	  \[
1580	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1581	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1582	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1583	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1584	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1585	  \]
1586	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1587	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1588	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1589	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1590	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1591	
1592	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1593	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1594	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1595	\end{proof}

1676	  i.e. \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\) for all \(r \in R\), \(m \in M\). If

1683	  \(m = \sum_i r_i t_i\) (\(t_i \in T\)); then

1685	  \(R'\)-combination of \(e(T)\). Hence the top submodule of \(M'\) is finitely
1686	  generated, i.e. \(M'\) is a finite \(R'\)-module.

1698	  \(\sigma\), with index set \(I = \sigma.I\). Then the section module
1699	  \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, D)\). This is the affine base
1700	  case of \(G1\): once the generators are in hand on an affine open, finiteness of
1701	  sections is purely an affine (Spec-side) statement. (Only quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is
1702	  needed — the finite-generation datum is carried by \(\sigma\) directly, so no separate
1703	  finite-type hypothesis on \(\F\) is required.)
1704	\end{lemma}
1705	
1706	\begin{proof}
1707	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
1708	        lem:module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1709	  The content is the transport of the finite generating datum
1710	  from the category of sheaves of modules over the affine open \(D\) to the affine
1711	  scheme \(\Spec \Gamma(X, D)\), where the affine base case applies. Write
1712	  \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\) and use the canonical identification
1713	  \(D \cong \Spec R\) (\(\mathrm{isoSpec}\)). The argument has three steps.
1714	
1715	  \emph{(a) Quasi-coherence on \(\Spec R\).} Transport \(\F|_D\) along
1716	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) to a sheaf of modules \(\F'\) on \(\Spec R\). Finite type of
1717	  \(\F\) is inherited by the restriction, and quasi-coherence of \(\F'\) is the assertion
1718	  that its counit \(\F'.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism; this is the affine
1719	  quasi-coherence criterion \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ} fed by the basic-open
1720	  localization datum \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1721	
1722	  \emph{(b) A finite free epimorphism over \(\Spec R\).} The generating family \(\sigma\)
1723	  furnishes an epimorphism \(\OO_D^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_D\). Transporting along
1724	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) and using that the pullback of the free module along an
1725	  isomorphism is again free (\((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1})^{*}\OO_D^{\oplus I}
1726	  \cong \OO_{\Spec R}^{\oplus I}\)), together with the identification of the free sheaf with
1727	  the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\) of \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) (the associated-sheaf
1728	  functor preserves coproducts and \(\widetilde{R} \cong \OO_{\Spec R}\)), gives an
1729	  epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) with \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) a finite
1730	  \(R\)-module (\(I\) finite).
1731	
1732	  \emph{(c) Identification of section modules.} The affine global-sections functor sends
1733	  \(\F'\) to \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\), which the transport identifies with \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)
1734	  as \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\)-modules.
1735	
1736	  Now the affine base case \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi}, applied to the finite-free
1737	  quasi-coherent epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) over \(\Spec R\),
1738	  shows \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\) is a finite \(R\)-module; by (c) this is \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)
1739	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, D)\).
1740	\end{proof}
1741	
1742	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1743	[G1 — finite-type quasi-coherent sheaf has finite affine sections]
1744	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1746	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover,
1747	        lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated,
1748	        lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi,
1749	        lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1750	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
1751	  % (Lemma "Characterizing modules of finite type", \S "Characterizing modules of
1752	  % finite type and finite presentation")
1753	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2092-L2098).
1754	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1755	  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
1756	  %  The quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1757	  %  $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1758	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1759	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1760	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1761	  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
1762	  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1763	\end{lemma}
1764	
1765	\begin{proof}
1766	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover,
1767	        lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated,
1768	        lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi,
1769	        lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1770	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
1771	  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
1772	  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
1773	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1774	  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\). Cover extraction
1775	  \cref{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated} gives a finite
1776	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) spanning the unit ideal, with each \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\))
1777	  affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) globally generated by finitely many sections, equivalently a
1778	  finite-free epimorphism \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\). The
1779	  free-epi base case \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi} applied on each affine \(D(g)\)
1780	  makes \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). The locality reduction
1781	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover} then glues these per-member
1782	  finiteness statements to give \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1783	\end{proof}

1940	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1941	[B1.0 — localization commutes with base tensor]
1942	  \label{lem:gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}
1943	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}
1944	  Let \(R \to B\) be a ring map, \(T \subseteq B\) a submonoid, \(N\) a \(B\)-module,
1945	  and \(K\) an \(R\)-module. There is an isomorphism, natural in \(K\),
1946	  \[
1947	    \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,(N \otimes_R K)
1948	      \;\cong\; (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K .
1949	  \]
1950	\end{lemma}
1951	\begin{proof}
1952	  Localization is base change: \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,M \cong T^{-1}B \otimes_B M\)
1953	  naturally in the \(B\)-module \(M\). Applying this to \(M = N \otimes_R K\) and using
1954	  associativity/commutativity of the tensor product over the tower
1955	  \(R \to B \to T^{-1}B\) gives
1956	  \(T^{-1}B \otimes_B (N \otimes_R K) \cong (T^{-1}B \otimes_B N) \otimes_R K
1957	  \cong (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K\); naturality in \(K\) is inherited
1958	  from the tensor functor.
1959	\end{proof}
