1961	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1962	[B1 — localizing the source ring preserves flatness over the base]
1963	  \label{lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}
1964	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}
1965	  \uses{lem:gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}
1966	  Let \(R \to B \to N\) be a scalar tower in which \(N\) is a \(B\)-module, and let
1967	  \(T \subseteq B\) be a submonoid of the \emph{source} ring \(B\). View \(N\) as an
1968	  \(R\)-module through the tower. If \(N\) is flat over \(R\), then the localized module
1969	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N\) is again flat over \(R\). \emph{(Note: the existing
1970	  Mathlib results \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves} localize over a
1971	  submonoid of the \emph{base} \(R\); here the submonoid lives in the source \(B\) while
1972	  the base \(R\) is untouched, so a separate argument is required.)}
1973	\end{lemma}
1974	
1975	\begin{proof}
1976	  \uses{lem:gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm, lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves}
1977	  Localization at \(T\) commutes with tensoring against an \(R\)-module: by
1978	  \cref{lem:gf_localizedModule_baseChange_tensor_comm}, for every \(R\)-module \(K\)
1979	  there is a natural isomorphism
1980	  \[
1981	    \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,(N \otimes_R K)
1982	      \;\cong\; (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K,
1983	  \]
1984	  natural in \(K\) (compatible with the maps induced by any \(R\)-linear \(K \to K'\)).
1985	  Flatness of \(N\) over \(R\) means that for every injection \(K \hookrightarrow K'\) of
1986	  \(R\)-modules the induced map \(N \otimes_R K \to N \otimes_R K'\) is injective.
1987	  Localization at the submonoid \(T\) is an exact functor, so it preserves this
1988	  injectivity; transporting along the natural isomorphism turns the result into
1989	  injectivity of
1990	  \((\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K \to (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K'\).
1991	  As this holds for every injection of \(R\)-modules, \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N\)
1992	  is flat over \(R\).
1993	\end{proof}

2007	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2008	[B2.1 — cross-chart basic-open identity]
2009	  \label{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2010	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2011	  \uses{lem:mathlib_scheme_basicOpen_res}
2012	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(W, W_j \subseteq X\) two opens with overlap
2013	  \(O = W \sqcap W_j\), and let \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\), \(\bar g \in \Gamma(X, W_j)\) be sections
2014	  that restrict to the \emph{same} section over \(O\), i.e.\ \(g|_O = \bar g|_O\) in
2015	  \(\Gamma(X, O)\). Suppose both basic opens lie in the overlap,
2016	  \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g \le O\) and \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g \le O\). Then they agree as
2017	  opens of \(X\):
2018	  \[
2019	    X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g \;=\; X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g .
2020	  \]
2021	  In particular the open \(D(g)\) is computed identically whether read in the chart \(W\) or in
2022	  the chart \(W_j\), and \(D(g) = D(\bar g) \le W_j\) (no module content is involved).
2023	\end{lemma}
2024	
2025	\begin{proof}
2026	  \uses{lem:mathlib_scheme_basicOpen_res}
2027	  Basic opens are compatible with restriction (\cref{lem:mathlib_scheme_basicOpen_res}). For the
2028	  inclusion \(O \le W\), \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,(g|_O) = O \sqcap X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g\), and
2029	  since \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g \le O\) the meet collapses to
2030	  \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,(g|_O) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g\). Symmetrically, for \(O \le W_j\),
2031	  \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,(\bar g|_O) = O \sqcap X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g
2032	  = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\). The hypothesis \(g|_O = \bar g|_O\) makes the two left-hand
2033	  sides equal, so \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\).

2036	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2037	[B2.2 — two-leg section-localization transport]
2038	  \label{lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg}
2039	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_section_localization_twoleg}
2040	  \uses{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen,
2041	        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
2042	  In the situation of the generic-flatness assembly \cref{thm:generic_flatness}, fix an affine open
2043	  \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\), a patch \(W_j = \Spec B_j\) with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and a matched
2044	  pair \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\), \(\bar g \in B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) cutting out the same basic open
2045	  \(D := X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g \le W \sqcap W_j\) (as produced by
2046	  \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}). Then the single group \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) carries two
2047	  localized-module structures:
2048	  \begin{enumerate}
2049	    \item the section restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) as
2050	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\); and
2051	    \item the section restriction \(M_j \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) as
2052	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g)^{-1}\,M_j = (M_j)_{\bar g}\).
2053	  \end{enumerate}
2054	  Consequently \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\) canonically, by
2055	  uniqueness of localized modules.

2058	\begin{proof}
2059	  \uses{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen,
2060	        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
2061	  By \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq} the two recipes for the open agree,
2062	  \(D = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\), so \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) is one and the
2063	  same group whether \(D\) is read in chart \(W\) or in chart \(W_j\). Applying the quasi-coherent
2064	  section-localization datum \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} with ambient affine \(W\)
2065	  and element \(g\), the restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) is the localization at
2066	  \(\mathrm{powers}\,g\), giving (1). Applying the same datum with ambient affine \(W_j\) and element
2067	  \(\bar g\) (legitimate since \(D = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g \le W_j\)), the restriction
2068	  \(M_j \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) is the localization at \(\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g\), giving (2). Both maps
2069	  localize their respective sources onto the same target \(\Gamma(\F, D)\); the universal property of
2070	  localized modules (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) uniqueness) supplies the canonical isomorphism
2071	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\). Finiteness of \(M_j\) over \(B_j\)
2072	  is recorded by \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}.
2073	\end{proof}

2076	[B2.3 — base comparison: \(\Gamma(S,U)\) localizes \(A_f\)]

2100	  \(A_f \to R\) is the restriction along \(U \le D(f)\) and exhibits \(R\) as a localization of
2101	  \(A_f\) inside the fraction field of the domain \(A\).
2102	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GradedHilbertSerre.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
160	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
161	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
162	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
163	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
164	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
165	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
166	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
167	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
168	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
169	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
170	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
171	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
172	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
173	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
174	  %  the function
175	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
176	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
177	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
178	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
179	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
180	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
181	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
182	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
183	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
184	  \[
185	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
186	  \]
187	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
188	  the tensor product of sections
189	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
190	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
191	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
192	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
193	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
194	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
195	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
196	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
197	  polynomial ring).
198	\end{definition}
199	
200	\begin{definition}
201	[Section graded module]
202	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
204	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
205	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
206	  % Polynomials" (read from
207	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
209	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
210	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
211	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
212	  %  the function
213	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
214	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
215	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
216	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
217	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
218	  I.7.}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
220	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
221	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
222	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
223	  \[
224	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
225	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
226	  \]
227	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
228	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
229	  by the section tensor product
230	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
231	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
232	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
233	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
234	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
235	\end{definition}
236	
237	\begin{lemma}
238	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
239	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
241	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
242	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
243	  % Polynomials" (read from
244	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
245	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
246	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
247	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
248	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
249	  %  the function
250	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
251	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
252	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
253	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
254	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
255	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
256	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
257	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
258	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
259	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
260	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
261	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
262	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
263	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
264	\end{lemma}
265	
266	\begin{proof}
267	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
268	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
269	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
270	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
271	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
272	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
273	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
274	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
275	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
276	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
277	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
278	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
279	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
280	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
281	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
282	\end{proof}
283	
284	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
285	  graded series]
286	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
287	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
288	  \mathlibok
289	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
290	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
291	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
292	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
293	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
294	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
295	  equals \(h(n)\):
296	  \[
297	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
298	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
299	  \]
300	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
301	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
302	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
303	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
304	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
305	\end{lemma}
306	
307	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
308	  (rank--nullity)]
309	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
310	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
311	  \mathlibok
312	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
313	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
314	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
315	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
316	  \[
317	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
318	  \]
319	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
320	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
321	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
322	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
323	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
324	\end{lemma}
325	
326	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
327	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
328	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
329	  \mathlibok
330	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
331	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
332	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
333	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
334	  inverse
335	  \[
336	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
337	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
338	  \]
339	  Together with the coercion
340	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
341	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
342	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
343	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
344	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
345	\end{lemma}

347	\begin{lemma}\leanok
348	[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
349	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
350	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
351	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
352	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
353	    lem:ratHilb_bump, def:graded_subquotientHilb,
354	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
355	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
356	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
357	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
358	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
359	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
360	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
361	  %  function
362	  %  $$
363	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
364	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
365	  %  $$
366	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
367	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
368	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
369	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
370	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
371	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
372	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
373	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
374	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
375	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
376	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
377	  such that for all \(n > N\)
378	  \[
379	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
380	  \]
381	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
382	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
383	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
384	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
385	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
386	
387	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
388	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
389	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
390	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
391	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
392	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
393	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
394	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
395	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
396	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
397	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
398	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
399	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
400	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
401	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
404	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
405	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
406	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
407	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
408	  %   and axiom-clean; the present theorem is a closed, axiom-clean Lean declaration
409	  %   (gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational). The ambient subquotient route
410	  %   (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps missing graded-quotient API by
411	  %   working with ambient families of submodules of \(M\).
412	\end{lemma}
413	
414	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
415	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
416	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
417	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
418	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
419	%  following short exact sequence
420	%  $$
421	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
422	%  $$
423	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
424	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
425	\begin{proof}
426	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
427	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
428	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
429	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
430	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
431	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
432	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
433	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
434	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
435	    \leanok
436	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
437	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
438	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
439	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
440	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
441	
442	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
443	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
444	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
445	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
446	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
447	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
448	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
449	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
450	  is the number on which we induct.
451	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
452	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
453	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
454	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
455	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
456	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
457	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
458	
459	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
460	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
461	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
462	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
463	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
464	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
465	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
466	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
467	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
468	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
469	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
470	
471	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
472	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
473	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
474	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
475	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
476	  \[
477	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
478	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
479	  \]
480	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
481	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
482	  with
483	  \[
484	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
485	    \qquad (n > N),
486	  \]
487	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
488	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
489	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
490	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
491	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
492	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
493	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
494	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
495	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
496	\end{proof}

1655	  \(U := \texttt{affineChart}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}),
1656	  \(V := \texttt{chartOverlap}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap}),
1657	  \(f := \texttt{chartIncl}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}),
1658	  \(t := \texttt{chartTransition}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}),

1662	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}} := \texttt{chartTransition'\_fac}\)

1667	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1668	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1669	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1670	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1671	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1672	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1673	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1674	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1675	  follows.
1676	\end{definition}

1678	\section{Separatedness}
1679	\label{sec:gr_separated}
1680	
1681	The surjectivity of the restricted-diagonal comorphism is the ring-theoretic
1682	heart of separatedness. We isolate it through the following sequence of
1683	helper results before stating the keystone lemma.
1684	
1685	\begin{lemma}
1686	
1687	\leanok
1688	[Two-sided inverse identity \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\,P^I_J = 1\)]
1689	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
1690	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
1691	  \uses{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
1692	  In \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), the pre-localisation transition hom
1693	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) satisfies the explicit
1694	  two-sided inverse identity
1695	  \[
1696	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_I\bigr) \cdot P^I_J \;=\; 1 .
1697	  \]
1698	  This refines the unit assertion of \cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit} to a named
1699	  inverse: \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\) is the explicit inverse of \(P^I_J\) in
1700	  \(R^I_J\).
1701	\end{lemma}
1702	\begin{proof}
1703	  \uses{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
1704	  \leanok
1705	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet} (taking the third subset \(K = I\)),
1706	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\) is the determinant of the \(I\)-minor of the image
1707	  matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\), which by
1708	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap} equals
1709	  \(\det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr)\). Hence
1710	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\), and multiplying by \(P^I_J =
1711	  \det(X^I_J)\) gives \(\det(X^I_J)^{-1} \cdot \det(X^I_J) = 1\) by the
1712	  localisation-inverse cancellation for the unit \(\det(X^I_J)\)
1713	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}).
1714	\end{proof}
1715	
1716	\begin{definition}
1717	
1718	\leanok
1719	[Restricted-diagonal comorphism \(\delta_{I,J}\)]
1720	  \label{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1721	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.diagonalRingMap}
1722	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre}
1723	  The \emph{restricted-diagonal comorphism} on the patch \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
1724	  U^J\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism
1725	  \[
1726	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
1727	    \longrightarrow R^I_J,
1728	    \qquad
1729	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad
1730	    1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1731	  \]
1732	  whose surjectivity is the content of separatedness; this is the \(\delta_{I,J}\)
1733	  of \cref{lem:gr_separated}.
1734	\end{definition}
1735	\begin{proof}
1736	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre}
1737	  \leanok
1738	  Construct \(\delta_{I,J}\) as the tensor-product lift of the two commuting
1739	  factors: the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] = R^I \to R^I_J\) on the left
1740	  factor, and the pre-localisation transition hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
1741	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) on the right factor. Since both land in the
1742	  commutative ring \(R^I_J\), the universal property of the tensor product of
1743	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras yields a unique \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism out
1744	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]\) restricting to these
1745	  factors; by construction its right component is \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\).
1746	\end{proof}
1747	
1748	\begin{lemma}
1749	
1750	\leanok
1751	[Left component of \(\delta_{I,J}\)]
1752	  \label{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_left}
1753	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.diagonalRingMap_left}
1754	  \uses{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1755	  For \(a \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the comorphism \(\delta_{I,J}\)
1756	  (\cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}) acts on the left factor by the structure map:
1757	  \[
1758	    \delta_{I,J}(a \otimes 1) \;=\; \mathrm{alg}_{R^I \to R^I_J}(a),
1759	  \]
1760	  the image of \(a\) under \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] = R^I \to R^I_J\).
1761	\end{lemma}
1762	\begin{proof}
1763	  \uses{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1764	  \leanok
1765	  Immediate from the defining tensor-product lift of
1766	  \cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}: the value on a left elementary tensor \(a \otimes
1767	  1\) is the left factor evaluated at \(a\), i.e.\ the structure map.
1768	\end{proof}
1769	
1770	\begin{lemma}
1771	
1772	\leanok
1773	[Right component of \(\delta_{I,J}\)]
1774	  \label{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_right}
1775	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.diagonalRingMap_right}
1776	  \uses{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1777	  For \(b \in \mathbb{Z}[X^J]\), the comorphism \(\delta_{I,J}\)
1778	  (\cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}) acts on the right factor by the pre-localisation
1779	  transition hom:
1780	  \[
1781	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes b) \;=\; \tilde\theta_{I,J}(b)
1782	  \]
1783	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}).
1784	\end{lemma}
1785	\begin{proof}
1786	  \uses{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1787	  \leanok
1788	  Immediate from the defining tensor-product lift of
1789	  \cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}: the value on a right elementary tensor \(1 \otimes
1790	  b\) is the right factor \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) evaluated at \(b\).
1791	\end{proof}
1792	
1793	\begin{lemma}
1794	
1795	\leanok
1796	[The restricted-diagonal comorphism is surjective]
1797	  \label{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective}
1798	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.diagonalRingMap_surjective}
1799	  \uses{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_left, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_right, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
1800	  The comorphism \(\delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
1801	  \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}) is surjective.
1802	\end{lemma}
1803	\begin{proof}
1804	  \uses{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_left, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_right, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
1805	  \leanok
1806	  Let \(z \in R^I_J\). Since \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) is the
1807	  localisation of \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) away from \(P^I_J\), surjectivity of
1808	  the localisation map provides \(a \in R^I\) and \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) with
1809	  \[
1810	    z \cdot (P^I_J)^n \;=\; \mathrm{alg}_{R^I \to R^I_J}(a).
1811	  \]
1812	  Consider the witness \(a \otimes (P^J_I)^n \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]
1813	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]\). Using multiplicativity together with
1814	  \cref{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_left} and \cref{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_right},
1815	  \[
1816	    \delta_{I,J}\bigl(a \otimes (P^J_I)^n\bigr)
1817	      = \mathrm{alg}_{R^I \to R^I_J}(a) \cdot \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)^n
1818	      = z \cdot (P^I_J)^n \cdot \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)^n
1819	      = z \cdot \bigl(P^I_J \cdot \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\bigr)^n
1820	      = z,
1821	  \]
1822	  where the last equality uses \(P^I_J \cdot \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1\) from
1823	  \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}. Hence \(z\) lies in the image
1824	  of \(\delta_{I,J}\), so \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective.
1825	\end{proof}
1826	
1827	\begin{definition}
1828	
1829	\leanok
1830	[Source pullback iso \(e_2\) for the diagonal computation]
1831	  \label{def:gr_pullbackιIso}
1832	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.pullbackιIso}
1833	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_chart_overlap}
1834	  The \emph{source pullback iso} \(e_2\) identifies the categorical fibre product
1835	  of two chart inclusions of the glued scheme (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) with
1836	  the overlap chart:
1837	  \[
1838	    e_2 : \mathrm{pullback}\,(\iota_i)\,(\iota_j) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1839	    U^I \cap U^J \;=\; \texttt{chartOverlap}\,(d,r,i,j)
1840	  \]
1841	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap}), where \(\iota_i, \iota_j\) are the open
1842	  immersions of the charts \(U^I, U^J\) into \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1843	\end{definition}
1844	\begin{proof}
1845	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_chart_overlap}
1846	  \leanok
1847	  The glue data of \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} supplies, for each pair \((i,j)\), a
1848	  pullback cone over \(\iota_i, \iota_j\) with apex the overlap chart \(U^I \cap
1849	  U^J\), and this cone is a limit cone. Comparing it with the categorical
1850	  pullback limit cone via the uniqueness-up-to-isomorphism of limit cone points
1851	  yields the desired isomorphism \(e_2\).
1852	\end{proof}
1853	
1854	\begin{definition}
1855	
1856	\leanok
1857	[Structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)]
1858	  \label{def:gr_to_specZ}
1859	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.toSpecZ}
1860	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme}
1861	  The \emph{structure morphism} of the glued Grassmannian scheme
1862	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is the canonical map
1863	  \[
1864	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z} .
1865	  \]
1866	  As \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lives in the category of schemes over \(\mathbb{Z}\), in
1867	  which \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is the terminal object, this morphism is the unique
1868	  map to that terminal object. It is the genuine base over which separatedness and
1869	  properness of the Grassmannian are formulated.
1870	\end{definition}
1871	
1872	\begin{lemma}
1873	
1874	\leanok
1875	[The chart map composes to the affine structure map]
1876	  \label{lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ}
1877	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.ι_toSpecZ}
1878	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, def:gr_glued_scheme}
1879	  For each index \(i = (I, \cdot)\), the chart immersion \(\iota_i : U^I \to
1880	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) of the glue datum (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) composed with
1881	  the structure morphism (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) is the affine structure map of
1882	  the chart:
1883	  \[
1884	    \iota_i \circ (\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z})
1885	      \;=\; \Spec(\mathbb{Z} \to R^I),
1886	  \]
1887	  the \(\Spec\) of the canonical ring map \(\mathbb{Z} \to R^I =
1888	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
1889	\end{lemma}
1890	\begin{proof}
1891	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, def:gr_glued_scheme}
1892	  \leanok
1893	  Both sides are morphisms from \(U^I\) into the terminal object \(\Spec
1894	  \mathbb{Z}\), hence they coincide by uniqueness of the terminal map.
1895	\end{proof}
1896	
1897	\begin{lemma}
1898	
1899	\leanok
1900	[First projection leg of the source pullback iso]
1901	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_fst}
1902	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.pullbackιIso_inv_fst}
1903	  \uses{def:gr_pullbackιIso, def:gr_chart_incl}
1904	  The inverse of the source pullback iso \(e_2\) (\cref{def:gr_pullbackιIso}),
1905	  followed by the first projection of the fibre product, is the chart inclusion:
1906	  \[
1907	    e_2^{-1} \circ \mathrm{pr}_1 \;=\; \iota^I_J : U^I_J \to U^I ,
1908	  \]
1909	  the \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) leg being the projection of
1910	  \(\mathrm{pullback}\,(\iota_i)\,(\iota_j)\) onto \(U^I\)
1911	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}).
1912	\end{lemma}
1913	\begin{proof}
1914	  \uses{def:gr_pullbackιIso, def:gr_chart_incl}
1915	  \leanok
1916	  By construction \(e_2\) is the comparison isomorphism between the categorical
1917	  pullback limit cone and the glue-datum pullback cone over \(\iota_i, \iota_j\).
1918	  The compatibility of a limit-cone comparison isomorphism with the cone legs
1919	  (uniqueness-up-to-isomorphism of limit cone points, applied to the left leg of
1920	  the cospan) identifies \(e_2^{-1} \circ \mathrm{pr}_1\) with the corresponding
1921	  leg of the glue-datum cone, which is the chart inclusion \(\iota^I_J\).
1922	\end{proof}
1923	
1924	\begin{lemma}
1925	
1926	\leanok
1927	[Second projection leg of the source pullback iso]
1928	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_snd}
1929	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.pullbackιIso_inv_snd}
1930	  \uses{def:gr_pullbackιIso, def:gr_chart_incl, def:gr_chart_transition}
1931	  The inverse of the source pullback iso \(e_2\) (\cref{def:gr_pullbackιIso}),
1932	  followed by the second projection of the fibre product, is the scheme-level
1933	  transition composed with the opposite chart inclusion:
1934	  \[
1935	    e_2^{-1} \circ \mathrm{pr}_2
1936	      \;=\; t_{I,J} \circ \iota^J_I : U^I_J \to U^J_I \to U^J ,
1937	  \]
1938	  the transition \(t_{I,J}\) of \cref{def:gr_chart_transition} followed by the
1939	  chart inclusion of \cref{def:gr_chart_incl}.
1940	\end{lemma}
1941	\begin{proof}
1942	  \uses{def:gr_pullbackιIso, def:gr_chart_incl, def:gr_chart_transition}
1943	  \leanok
1944	  As in \cref{lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_fst}, but using the right leg of the cospan:
1945	  the glue-datum pullback cone's second leg is the composite \(t \circ f\) of the
1946	  glue datum (transition followed by inclusion), so the comparison isomorphism
1947	  identifies \(e_2^{-1} \circ \mathrm{pr}_2\) with \(t_{I,J} \circ \iota^J_I\).
1948	\end{proof}
1949	
1950	\begin{lemma}
1951	
1952	\leanok
1953	[The transition-then-inclusion composite is affine]
1954	  \label{lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
1955	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
1956	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition, def:gr_chart_incl, def:gr_transition_pre}
1957	  The composite of the scheme-level transition
1958	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}) with the opposite chart inclusion
1959	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}) is the \(\Spec\) of the pre-localisation transition
1960	  homomorphism \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : R^J \to R^I[1/P^I_J]\)
1961	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}):
1962	  \[
1963	    t_{I,J} \circ \iota^J_I \;=\; \Spec \tilde\theta_{I,J} :
1964	      U^I_J \to U^J .
1965	  \]
1966	\end{lemma}
1967	\begin{proof}
1968	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition, def:gr_chart_incl, def:gr_transition_pre}
1969	  \leanok
1970	  Unfolding the two scheme maps, the composite is \(\Spec\) of the ring map
1971	  obtained by precomposing the localised transition \(\theta_{I,J} :
1972	  R^J[1/P^J_I] \to R^I[1/P^I_J]\) with the localisation \(R^J \to R^J[1/P^J_I]\).
1973	  By the universal property of the away-localisation (the lift commutes with the
1974	  localisation map), this composite ring map is exactly the pre-localisation
1975	  transition \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\); applying \(\Spec\) and the contravariance of
1976	  composition gives the claim.
1977	\end{proof}
1978	
1979	\begin{lemma}
1980	
1981	\leanok
1982	[The structure morphism is separated (morphism form)]
1983	  \label{lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
1984	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparatedToSpecZ}
1985	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_pullbackιIso,
1986	    def:gr_diagonalRingMap, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective,
1987	    lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ, lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_fst,
1988	    lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_snd, lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
1989	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1990	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) is separated: its diagonal is a closed immersion.
1991	\end{lemma}
1992	\begin{proof}
1993	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_pullbackιIso,
1994	    def:gr_diagonalRingMap, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective,
1995	    lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ, lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_fst,
1996	    lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_snd, lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
1997	    \leanok
1998	  Separatedness is local on the target of the diagonal, so it may be checked on
1999	  the cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) by the
2000	  patch products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) (the left--right product of the
2001	  chart open cover with itself). Fix a patch \((i,j)\). On it the restricted
2002	  diagonal is identified, via the source pullback iso \(e_2\)
2003	  (\cref{def:gr_pullbackιIso}) — whose two legs are computed in
2004	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_fst} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackιIso_inv_snd},
2005	  using \cref{lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ} and \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_comp_chartIncl}
2006	  to read off the affine comorphisms — and the target affine iso (the pullback of
2007	  two affine schemes is the \(\Spec\) of the tensor product), with the affine
2008	  morphism \(\Spec \delta_{I,J}\), where \(\delta_{I,J}\) is the
2009	  restricted-diagonal comorphism of \cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}. Since
2010	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective (\cref{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective}), the
2011	  morphism \(\Spec \delta_{I,J}\) is a closed immersion. As every patch of the
2012	  diagonal is a closed immersion, the diagonal is a closed immersion and the
2013	  structure morphism is separated.
2014	\end{proof}
2015	
2016	\begin{lemma}
2017	
2018	\leanok
2019	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
2020	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
2021	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
2022	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective, lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2023	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
2024	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
2025	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2026	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
2027	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
2028	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
2029	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
2030	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2031	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2032	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
2033	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
2034	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
2035	\end{lemma}
2036	
2037	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
2038	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
2039	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
2040	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
2041	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
2042	\begin{proof}
2043	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective, lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2044	  \leanok
2045	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
2046	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
2047	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
2048	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
2049	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
2050	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
2051	  homomorphism is surjective.
2052	
2053	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
2054	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
2055	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
2056	  \[
2057	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
2058	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
2059	    \qquad
2060	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
2061	  \]
2062	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
2063	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
2064	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
2065	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
2066	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
2067	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
2068	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
2069	  and in particular
2070	  \[
2071	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
2072	  \]
2073	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
2074	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
2075	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
2076	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
2077	  \[
2078	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
2079	  \]
2080	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
2081	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
2082	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
2083	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
2084	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
2085	\end{proof}
2086	
2087	\section{Properness}
2088	\label{sec:gr_proper}
2089	
2090	Following Nitsure \S 1, the structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec
2091	\mathbb{Z}\) is proper. We reduce properness to the \emph{existence} half of the
2092	valuative criterion: the Mathlib criterion \texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion}
2093	reduces \(\mathrm{IsProper}\,\pi\) to four ingredients --- quasi-compactness,
2094	quasi-separatedness, local finiteness of type, and the valuative criterion
2095	\(\mathrm{Existence} \sqcap \mathrm{Uniqueness}\). The first three and the
2096	uniqueness half are cheap (uniqueness is free from separatedness), so the only
2097	genuine content is \(\mathrm{Existence}\): every commutative square from a
2098	valuation ring admits a diagonal filler. This existence statement is the Nitsure
2099	chart-selection argument, which we decompose into four steps E1--E4.
2100	
2101	\subsection*{Cheap ingredients and the reduction to existence}
2102	
2103	\begin{lemma}
2104	
2105	\leanok
2106	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a compact space]
2107	  \label{lem:gr_compactSpace_scheme}
2108	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.compactSpace_scheme}
2109	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_glued_scheme}
2110	  The underlying topological space of the glued Grassmannian scheme
2111	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is quasi-compact: it carries the
2112	  structure of a \texttt{CompactSpace}.
2113	\end{lemma}
2114	\begin{proof}
2115	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_affine_chart}
2116	  \leanok
2117	  The glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) is indexed by the finite set
2118	  \(\{\, I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\} : \#(I) = d \,\}\) of size-\(d\) subsets, and
2119	  each chart \(U^I = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is the
2120	  \(\Spec\) of a ring, hence quasi-compact. A scheme glued from finitely many
2121	  quasi-compact charts is quasi-compact, so \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a compact
2122	  space.
2123	\end{proof}
2124	
2125	\begin{lemma}
2126	
2127	\leanok
2128	[The structure morphism is quasi-compact]
2129	  \label{lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ}
2130	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.quasiCompact_toSpecZ}
2131	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, lem:gr_compactSpace_scheme}
2132	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2133	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) is quasi-compact.
2134	\end{lemma}
2135	\begin{proof}
2136	  \uses{lem:gr_compactSpace_scheme}
2137	  \leanok
2138	  The target \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is affine, so quasi-compactness of \(\pi\) is
2139	  equivalent to quasi-compactness of the total space \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), which is
2140	  a compact space by \cref{lem:gr_compactSpace_scheme}.
2141	\end{proof}
2142	
2143	\begin{lemma}
2144	
2145	\leanok
2146	[The structure morphism is locally of finite type]
2147	  \label{lem:gr_locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ}
2148	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ}
2149	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_affine_chart, lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ}
2150	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2151	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) is locally of finite type.
2152	\end{lemma}
2153	\begin{proof}
2154	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ}
2155	  \leanok
2156	  Being locally of finite type is local on the source, so it may be checked on the
2157	  chart open cover (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}). On the chart \(U^I\) the composite
2158	  \(U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \xrightarrow{\pi} \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is, by
2159	  \cref{lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ}, the \(\Spec\) of the structure map \(\mathbb{Z} \to R^I
2160	  = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\). The polynomial ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is a finitely
2161	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra, so this map is of finite type; hence \(\pi\) is
2162	  locally of finite type.
2163	\end{proof}
2164	
2165	\begin{lemma}
2166	
2167	\leanok
2168	[The structure morphism is quasi-separated]
2169	  \label{lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ}
2170	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.quasiSeparated_toSpecZ}
2171	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}

2173	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) is quasi-separated.
2174	\end{lemma}
2175	\begin{proof}

2181	
2182	\begin{lemma}
2183	
2184	\leanok
2185	[Uniqueness half of the valuative criterion]
2186	  \label{lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ}

2188	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2189	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2190	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) satisfies the \emph{uniqueness} part of the valuative
2191	  criterion: for a valuation ring \(R\) with fraction field \(K\), any two
2192	  morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\) that agree
2193	  on the generic point \(\Spec K\) coincide.

2199	  uniqueness half of the valuative criterion is free for any separated morphism:

2201	  coincide because the diagonal is a (closed, in particular separated) immersion.
2202	\end{proof}
2203	
2204	\begin{lemma}

2206	\leanok
2207	[Properness from the existence half of the valuative criterion]
2208	  \label{lem:gr_isProper_of_valuativeExistence}
2209	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper_of_valuativeExistence}
2210	  \uses{lem:gr_compactSpace_scheme, lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ,
2211	    lem:gr_locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ, lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ,
2212	    lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ, def:gr_to_specZ}
2213	  Suppose the structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2214	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) satisfies the \emph{existence} part of the valuative
2215	  criterion. Then \(\pi\) is proper.
2216	\end{lemma}
2217	\begin{proof}
2218	  \uses{lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ, lem:gr_locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ,
2219	    lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ, lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ}
2220	    \leanok
2221	  The Mathlib criterion \texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion} reduces properness
2222	  of \(\pi\) to four hypotheses: \(\pi\) is quasi-compact
2223	  (\cref{lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ}), quasi-separated
2224	  (\cref{lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ}), locally of finite type
2225	  (\cref{lem:gr_locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ}), and satisfies the full valuative
2226	  criterion. The full valuative criterion is the conjunction of its existence and
2227	  uniqueness halves; the uniqueness half holds by
2228	  \cref{lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ}, and the existence half is the
2229	  hypothesis. Assembling the four ingredients gives properness.
2230	\end{proof}
2231	
2232	\subsection*{The existence obligation: chart selection by minimal valuation}
2233	
2234	The existence half of the valuative criterion is the genuine geometric content,
2235	and it is exactly Nitsure's chart-selection argument. A commutative square over a
2236	valuation ring presents a valuation ring \(R\) with fraction field \(K =
2237	\operatorname{Frac}(R)\), valuation \(v := \mathrm{val}_R : K \to \Gamma\) (written

2241	\(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) making both triangles commute. We carry this out
2242	in four steps. The algebraic core --- the behaviour of the transition hom on a
2243	\(K'\)-minor --- is the following identity over the localised chart ring.

2421	        lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg, lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat,
