2423	        lem:gf_flat_descend_isEpi, lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span,
2424	        lem:mathlib_flat_of_free, lem:mathlib_free_of_isLocalizedModule}
2425	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
2426	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
2427	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
2428	
2429	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact and
2430	  \(U_0\) is affine, hence quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is
2431	  quasi-compact; choose a
2432	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
2433	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
2434	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
2435	
2436	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
2437	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
2438	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
2439	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
2440	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
2441	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
2442	
2443	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
2444	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
2445	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
2446	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
2447	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
2448	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
2449	
2450	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness (source-span descent with base descent along
2451	  \(U \hookrightarrow V\)).} On each patch the localized section module \((M_j)_f\) is free,
2452	  hence flat over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\). We promote these per-patch freeness statements to
2453	  the flatness conclusion for every affine \(U \le V\) and affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\), with
2454	  \(R := \Gamma(S, U)\), by a \emph{source-span} descent on \(W\).
2455	
2456	  Cover \(W\) by basic opens aligned to the patches: by the patch-aligned covering lemma
2457	  \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover} there is a finite family
2458	  \(\{g\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,\{g\} = \top\), each
2459	  \(D(g) \subseteq W \cap W_{j}\) a common basic open matched by a patch element
2460	  \(\bar g \in B_{j}\) (so \(D(g) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\)). For each such \(g\): the
2461	  two-leg transport \cref{lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg} identifies the section module
2462	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) \cong (M_{j})_{\bar g}\). The per-patch freeness gives \((M_{j})_f\)
2463	  free, hence flat over \(A_f\) (\cref{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat},
2464	  \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_free}); since \(D(g)\) lies over \(U \le V = D(f)\) the image of
2465	  \(f\) is already invertible on \(D(\bar g)\), so localizing \((M_{j})_f\) at the
2466	  powers of \(\bar g\) keeps \(A_f\)-flatness
2467	  (\cref{lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}) and yields exactly \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) flat
2468	  over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\).
2469	
2470	  \emph{Base descent along the open immersion.} The inclusion \(U \hookrightarrow V\) of
2471	  affine opens makes \(\Gamma(S, V) \to \Gamma(S, U)\) a ring epimorphism
2472	  (\cref{lem:gf_openImmersion_isEpi}); flat descent along a ring epimorphism
2473	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_descend_isEpi} therefore upgrades ``\(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) flat over
2474	  \(\Gamma(S, V)\)'' to ``\(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) flat over \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\)''. Finally the
2475	  \(\{g\}\) span the unit ideal of \(\Gamma(X, W)\), so the source-span flatness criterion
2476	  \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span}, packaged as
2477	  \cref{lem:gf_section_span_flat_descent}, descends these flat principal localizations to
2478	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) flat over \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\). This holds for every affine \(U \le V\)
2479	  and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\), producing the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over
2480	  \(\OO_V\).
2481	\end{proof}

500	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
501	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
502	
503	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
504	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
505	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
506	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
507	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
508	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
509	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
510	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
511	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
512	
513	\begin{lemma}\leanok
514	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
515	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
516	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
517	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
518	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
519	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
520	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
521	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
522	  \(F\):
523	  \[
524	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
525	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
526	  \]
527	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
528	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
529	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
530	  the Cauchy product.
531	\end{lemma}
532	
533	\begin{lemma}\leanok
534	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
535	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
536	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
537	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
538	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
539	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
540	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
541	  \[
542	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
543	  \]
544	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
545	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
546	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
547	  \[
548	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
549	  \]
550	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
551	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
552	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
553	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
554	\end{lemma}
555	
556	\begin{proof}
557	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
558	  \leanok
559	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
560	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
561	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
562	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
563	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
564	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
565	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
566	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
567	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
568	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
569	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
570	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
571	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
572	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
573	\end{proof}
574	
575	\begin{definition}\leanok
576	[Rational Hilbert function]
577	  \label{def:ratHilb}
578	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
579	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
580	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
581	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
582	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
583	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
584	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
585	  \[
586	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
587	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
588	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
589	  \]
590	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
591	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
592	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
593	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
594	\end{definition}
595	
596	\begin{lemma}\leanok
597	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
598	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
599	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
600	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
601	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
602	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
603	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
604	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
605	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
606	\end{lemma}
607	
608	\begin{lemma}\leanok
609	[Raising the pole order]
610	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
611	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
612	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
613	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
614	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
615	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
616	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
617	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
618	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
619	\end{lemma}
620	
621	\begin{lemma}\leanok
622	[Closure under difference]
623	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
624	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
625	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
626	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
627	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
628	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
629	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
630	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
631	\end{lemma}
632	
633	\begin{lemma}\leanok
634	[Closure under right shift]
635	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
636	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
637	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
638	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
639	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
640	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
641	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
642	  Lean.
643	\end{lemma}
644	
645	\begin{lemma}\leanok
646	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
647	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
648	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
649	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
650	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
651	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
652	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
653	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
654	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
655	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
656	\end{lemma}
657	
658	\begin{proof}
659	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
660	  \leanok
661	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
662	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
663	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
664	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
665	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
666	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
667	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
668	\end{proof}
669	
670	\begin{lemma}\leanok
671	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
672	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
673	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
674	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
675	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
676	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
677	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
678	  \[
679	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
680	  \]
681	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
682	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
683	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
684	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
685	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
686	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
687	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
688	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
689	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
690	\end{lemma}
691	
692	\begin{proof}
693	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
694	  \leanok
695	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
696	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
697	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
698	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
699	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
700	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
701	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
702	\end{proof}
703	
704	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
705	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
706	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
707	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
708	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
709	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
710	  % Polynomials" (read from
711	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
712	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
713	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
714	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
715	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
716	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
717	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
718	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
719	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
720	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
721	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
722	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
723	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
724	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
725	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
726	  \[
727	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
728	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
729	  \]
730	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
731	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
732	\end{theorem}
733	
734	\begin{proof}
735	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
736	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
737	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
738	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
739	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
740	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
741	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
742	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
743	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
744	  function
745	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
746	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
747	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
748	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
749	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
750	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
751	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
752	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
753	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
754	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
755	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
756	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
757	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
758	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
759	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
760	\end{proof}

762	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
763	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
764	
765	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
766	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
767	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
768	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
769	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
770	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
771	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
772	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
773	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
774	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
775	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
776	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
777	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
778	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
779	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
780	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
781	
782	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
783	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
784	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
785	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
786	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
787	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
788	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
789	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
790	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
791	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
792	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
793	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
794	
795	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
796	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
797	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
798	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
799	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
800	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
801	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
802	\(x \in R_1\).
803	
804	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
805	
806	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
807	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
808	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
809	  \mathlibok
810	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
811	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
812	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
813	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
814	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
815	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
816	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
817	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
818	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
819	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
820	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
821	\end{lemma}
822	
823	\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
824	  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
825	  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
826	  \mathlibok
827	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
828	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
829	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
830	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
831	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
832	  is homogeneous, hence \(xM\) and the auxiliary submodule \(N' + x \cdot N\) are
833	  homogeneous; this homogeneity feeds the kernel/cokernel closure
834	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
838	  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
839	  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}
840	  \mathlibok
841	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
842	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.FiniteDimensional.Lemmas}).}
843	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
844	  \(\varphi : V \to W\) a \(\kappa\)-linear map. Then
845	  \[
846	    \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
847	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
848	  \]
849	  This is the rank--nullity identity underlying the degreewise difference identity
850	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}).
851	\end{lemma}
852	
853	\begin{lemma}[Finite generation transfers along a surjection]
854	  \label{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
855	  \lean{Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective}
856	  \mathlibok
857	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic}).}
858	  Let \(\varphi : R \to A\) be a surjective ring homomorphism and \(M\) an
859	  \(A\)-module that is finitely generated when viewed as an \(R\)-module via
860	  \(\varphi\). Then \(M\) is finitely generated as an \(A\)-module. Applied to the
861	  surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots,
862	  t_{r-2}]\) killing the endomorphism \(x = t_{r-1}\) that annihilates the two
863	  subquotients, this transfers their finite generation down one generator
864	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
865	\end{lemma}
866	
867	\begin{lemma}[Multiplication by a homogeneous element shifts degree]
868	  \label{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
869	  \lean{SetLike.IsHomogeneousElem.graded_smul}
870	  \mathlibok
871	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GradedMulAction}).}
872	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a graded family acting on a graded family \(\mathcal{M}\)
873	  via a graded scalar multiplication. If \(a \in R\) is homogeneous of degree
874	  \(i\) -- in particular \(x \in R_1\) -- then for \(m \in M_n\) one has
875	  \(a \cdot m \in M_{i+n}\). This is the degree-shifting property that makes \(xM\)
876	  a homogeneous submodule (with degree-\((n+1)\) part \(x \cdot M_n\)) and that
877	  underlies the ambient identity \(x \cdot N \cap M_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap M_n)\)
878	  used in the subquotient closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and
879	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}).
880	\end{lemma}
881	
882	\begin{lemma}[Commutative subalgebra generated by commuting elements]
883	  \label{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
884	  \lean{Algebra.isMulCommutative_adjoin}
885	  \mathlibok
886	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
887	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Algebra.Subalgebra.Lattice}).}
888	  Let \(R\) be a commutative semiring and \(A\) an \(R\)-algebra, possibly
889	  noncommutative. If \(s \subseteq A\) is a set of pairwise-commuting elements
890	  (\(a b = b a\) for all \(a, b \in s\)), then the \(R\)-subalgebra
891	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_R(s)\) generated by \(s\) has commutative multiplication; the
892	  commutative-ring structure on it follows. Applied to the \(r\) pairwise-commuting degree-raising endomorphisms
893	  \(x_0, \dots, x_{r-1} \in \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\), it yields a
894	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots,
895	  x_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); the polynomial action of
896	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) on \(M\) factors through this commutative \(A\)
897	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
898	\end{lemma}
899	
900	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness transfers along a surjective linear map]
901	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}
902	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_surjective}
903	  \mathlibok

914	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness descends along an injective linear map into a Noetherian module]
915	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_injective_mathlib}
916	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_injective}
917	  \mathlibok

926	  \label{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
927	  \lean{Submodule.liftQ}

932	  \(\varphi\) factors uniquely through a linear map \(M/P \to N\) on the quotient.

939	\begin{lemma}[Only finitely many members of an independent family are nonzero]
940	  \label{lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib}
941	  \lean{Submodule.finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep}
942	  \mathlibok

954	  \label{lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib}

979	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
980	
981	\begin{lemma}\leanok
982	[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
983	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
984	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
985	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
986	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
987	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
988	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
989	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
990	  \(n\),
991	  \[
992	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
993	  \]
994	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
995	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
996	\end{lemma}
997	
998	\begin{proof}
999	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}

2255	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L878-L883).
2256	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2257	  % "Now consider the homomorphism
2258	  %  $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
2259	  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
2260	  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
2261	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
2262	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$."
2263	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2264	  For size-\(d\) subsets \(I, J, K\), the pre-localisation transition hom
2265	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) satisfies, in \(R^I_J =
2266	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), the minor-ratio identity
2267	  \[
2268	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr)\cdot P^I_J \;=\; P^I_K
2269	  \]
2270	  (where \(P^I_J, P^I_K\) are read through the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
2271	  R^I_J\)). Equivalently \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) = P^I_K / P^I_J\), the
2272	  determinant of the \(K\)-minor of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). This generalises
2273	  \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul} (the case \(K = I\), where
2274	  \(P^I_I = 1\)) and is the algebraic engine of the existence argument: pulled back
2275	  through a \(K\)-point \(f\) it gives \(g(P^J_K) = f(P^I_K)/f(P^I_J)\).
2276	\end{lemma}
2277	\begin{proof}
2278	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
2279	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
2280	    \leanok
2281	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
2282	  \det(M_K)\) is the determinant of the \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M =
2283	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). Splitting the \(K\)-column submatrix of the product
2284	  (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) and using multiplicativity of the determinant,
2285	  \(\det(M_K) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr)\cdot \det(X^I_K) =
2286	  \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\cdot P^I_K\). Multiplying by \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) and
2287	  cancelling \(\det(X^I_J)^{-1}\det(X^I_J) = 1\)
2288	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}) gives
2289	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K)\cdot P^I_J = P^I_K\).
2290	\end{proof}

2295	[E1 --- the \(K\)-point factors through a single chart]
2296	  \label{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization}
2297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.existence_chart_factorization}
2298	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,

2300	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
2301	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L868-L870).
2302	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2303	  % "Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
2304	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by
2305	  %  a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$."
2306	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2307	  The morphism \(i_1 : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) factors through a single
2308	  chart: there exist a size-\(d\) subset \(I\) and a ring homomorphism \(f : R^I =
2309	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\) such that
2310	  \[
2311	    i_1 \;=\; \Spec(f) \circ \iota_I : \Spec K \to U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d),
2312	  \]
2313	  where \(\iota_I : U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is the chart immersion of the glue
2314	  datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}).
2315	\end{lemma}
2316	\begin{proof}
2317	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}
2318	  \leanok
2319	  Since \(K\) is a field, \(\Spec K\) is a one-point space, so its image point \(x
2320	  := i_1(\ast) \in \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a single point. The chart immersions
2321	  \(\{\iota_I : U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\}\) of the glue datum
2322	  (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) are jointly surjective (their images cover the
2323	  glued scheme \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}), so \(x\) lies in the range of some
2324	  \(\iota_I\). Each \(\iota_I\) is an open immersion
2325	  (\cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion} gives this for the chart inclusions, and
2326	  the glue datum's immersions inherit it). A morphism out of \(\Spec K\) whose
2327	  image point lies in the range of an open immersion factors uniquely through that
2328	  open immersion; hence \(i_1 = j \circ \iota_I\) for a unique \(j : \Spec K \to
2329	  U^I\). As \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) is affine, \(j = \Spec(f)\) for a unique ring map
2330	  \(f : R^I \to K\), giving the claimed factorization.
2331	\end{proof}

2347	  %  the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
2348	  %  $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore

2356	  Fix \(I\) and \(f : R^I \to K\) as in \cref{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization},
2357	  and let \(v := \mathrm{val}_R : K \to \Gamma\) be the (multiplicative) valuation
2358	  of \(R\). Over the finite index set \(\{\, J : \#(J) = d \,\}\) choose \(J\)
2359	  \emph{maximising} \(v\bigl(f(P^I_J)\bigr)\) (Nitsure's minimal additive valuation
2360	  \(\nu\) is the maximal multiplicative \(v\)). Then \(I\) itself is a candidate and
2361	  \(P^I_I = 1\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_self}) gives \(v(f(P^I_I)) = v(1) = 1\), so
2362	  the maximum satisfies \(v(f(P^I_J)) \ge 1 > 0\). In particular \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\),

2370	  \(P^I_I = 1\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_self}) and \(v(1) = 1\), the value at \(I\)

2398	[Free entry as a signed minor (E3 cofactor sub-step)]
2399	  \label{lem:gr_free_entry_eq_signed_minor}
2400	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.exists_minorDet_eq_free_entry}
2401	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_det_one_updateCol}
2402	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (the cofactor sub-step of the Properness argument).}
2403	  Fix a size-\(d\) subset \(J\), a row index \(p : \mathrm{Fin}\,d\), and a column \(q \notin J\). Put
2404	  \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\), again of size \(d\). Then there is an order-iso realisation of
2405	  \(K'\) under which the \(K'\)-minor of the universal matrix equals, up to sign, the free entry:
2406	  \[
2407	    \mathrm{minorDet}\,d\,r\,J\,K' \;=\; \pm\, X_{(p,\,q)} .
2408	  \]
2409	\end{lemma}
2410	\begin{proof}
2411	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_det_one_updateCol}
2412	  \leanok
2413	  Reorder the columns of \(K'\) so that column \(q\) sits in the \(p\)-th slot and the remaining columns
2414	  match \(J\) in increasing order; call \(\sigma\) the resulting permutation. Because the \(J\)-minor
2415	  \(X^J_J\) is the identity, the so-reordered submatrix is the identity with its \(p\)-th column overwritten
2416	  by the single free column carrying \(X_{(p,q)}\). The determinant of an identity matrix with one column
2417	  replaced by a vector \(v\) is exactly \(v_p\), with \emph{no} sign --- via Cramer's rule,
2418	  \(\det\bigl((1).\mathrm{updateCol}\,p\,v\bigr) = \bigl((1).\mathrm{cramer}\,v\bigr)_p
2419	  = (\mathrm{1}\cdot v)_p = v_p\). Restoring the increasing column order multiplies by \(\mathrm{sign}(\sigma)
2420	  = \pm 1\) (\(\mathrm{det\_permute}'\)), so \(\mathrm{minorDet}\,d\,r\,J\,K' = \mathrm{sign}(\sigma)\cdot
2421	  X_{(p,q)} = \pm X_{(p,q)}\).
2422	\end{proof}
2423	
2424	\begin{lemma}
2425	
2426	\leanok
2427	[E3 --- entries land in \(R\); \(g\) factors through the valuation ring]
2428	  \label{lem:gr_existence_factor_through_valuation_ring}
2429	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.existence_factor_through_valuationRing}
2430	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul, lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation,
2431	    lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross,
2432	    lem:gr_free_entry_eq_signed_minor,
2433	    lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det}
2434	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
2435	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L881-L887).
2436	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2437	  % "Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
2438	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
2439	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
2440	  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
2441	  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
2442	  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
2443	  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$."
2444	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). Set
2445	  %   `g := f' ∘ transitionPreMap d r I J`, where `f' : R^I_J → K` is the
2446	  %   `IsLocalization.Away.lift` of `f` along `minorDet d r I J` (a unit by E2).
2447	  %   Prove `∀ x, g x ∈ (algebraMap R K).range`, hence factor `g = (algebraMap R K)
2448	  %   ∘ g'` for a unique ring hom `g' : R^J → R`.
2449	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2450	  Define \(g : R^J = \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to K\) by the matrix formula \(g(X^J) =
2451	  f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr)\); concretely \(g = f' \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}\),
2452	  where \(f' : R^I_J \to K\) is the extension of \(f\) to the away-localisation
2453	  \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (well-defined by the universal property
2454	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}, since \(f(P^I_J)\) is a unit of \(K\) by
2455	  \cref{lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation}). Then every value \(g(P^J_K)\) lies in
2456	  \(R\), and consequently \(g\) factors uniquely as \(g = (R \hookrightarrow K)
2457	  \circ g'\) for a ring homomorphism \(g' : R^J \to R\). Moreover \(g\) defines the
2458	  same \(K\)-point as \(f\).
2459	\end{lemma}
2460	\begin{proof}
2461	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul, lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation,
2462	    lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
2463	    \leanok
2464	  Applying \(f'\) to the minor-ratio identity
2465	  (\cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul}), and using that \(f'\) extends
2466	  \(f\), gives for every size-\(d\) subset \(K'\)
2467	  \[
2468	    g\bigl(P^J_{K'}\bigr)\cdot f(P^I_J) \;=\; f(P^I_{K'}),
2469	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
2470	    g\bigl(P^J_{K'}\bigr) \;=\; \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)} .
2471	  \]
2472	  By the maximality in \cref{lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation},
2473	  \(v(f(P^I_{K'})) \le v(f(P^I_J))\), hence \(v\bigl(g(P^J_{K'})\bigr) =
2474	  v(f(P^I_{K'}))/v(f(P^I_J)) \le 1\), i.e.\ \(g(P^J_{K'}) \in R\). (That
2475	  \(f(P^I_J)\) is a unit and the cross minors map to units is recorded by
2476	  \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}, via the determinant identity of
2477	  \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}.) Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\)
2478	  is the identity, so cofactor expansion expresses each free entry \(x^J_{p,q}\)
2479	  (\(q \notin J\)) as, up to sign, the minor \(P^J_{K'}\) with \(K' = (J \setminus
2480	  \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) --- the determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th
2481	  identity column of \(X^J_J\) by column \(q\). Hence \(g(x^J_{p,q}) = \pm
2482	  g(P^J_{K'}) \in R\), while the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\),
2483	  already in \(R\). (This entry-as-minor sign bookkeeping is the one sub-step with
2484	  no pre-existing matrix-algebra scaffold; it requires expanding the determinant of
2485	  a column-substituted identity matrix.) Since \(g\) sends every generator of
2486	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]\) into \(R\), it factors uniquely through the subring \(R
2487	  \subseteq K\) as \(g = (R \hookrightarrow K) \circ g'\) with \(g' : R^J \to R\).
2488	\end{proof}

2490	\begin{definition}
2491	
2492	\leanok
2493	[Base-point lift along \((R \hookrightarrow K)\)]
2494	  \label{def:gr_liftToBaseOfMemRange}
2495	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.liftToBaseOfMemRange}
2496	  A ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to K\) into the fraction field \(K\) of a domain \(R\) whose
2497	  image lies in the subring \((R \hookrightarrow K)\) (i.e.\ \(\varphi(x) \in (\mathrm{algebraMap}\,R\,K)\)
2498	  for all \(x\)) corestricts to a ring homomorphism \(\varphi' : A \to R\). This packages the
2499	  factorisation \(g'\) of \cref{lem:gr_existence_factor_through_valuation_ring} in step E5: the
2500	  corestriction along the injective \(\mathrm{rangeRestrict}\) of \(R \hookrightarrow K\) (an iso onto
2501	  its range by \(\mathrm{IsFractionRing.injective}\)).
2502	\end{definition}
2503	
2504	\begin{lemma}
2505	
2506	\leanok
2507	[Defining property of the base-point lift]
2508	  \label{lem:gr_algebraMap_comp_liftToBaseOfMemRange}
2509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.algebraMap_comp_liftToBaseOfMemRange}
2510	  \uses{def:gr_liftToBaseOfMemRange}
2511	  Composing the corestriction \(\varphi'\) (\cref{def:gr_liftToBaseOfMemRange}) back with the
2512	  structure map \(R \hookrightarrow K\) recovers the original map:
2513	  \((\mathrm{algebraMap}\,R\,K) \circ \varphi' = \varphi\).
2514	\end{lemma}
2515	\begin{proof}
2516	
2517	\leanok
2518	  The corestriction inverts the range-restriction iso of \(R \hookrightarrow K\), so re-applying that
2519	  iso (i.e.\ post-composing with \(R \hookrightarrow K\)) returns \(\varphi\). Proved directly in Lean.
2520	\end{proof}

2522	\begin{lemma}
2523	
2524	\leanok
2525	[E3 ratio core --- the localised minor-ratio identity (coverage)]
2526	  \label{lem:gr_existence_lift_transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul}
2527	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.existence_lift_transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul}
2528	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul, lem:mathlib_away_lift}
2529	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure] \S1, the displayed minor-ratio of the Properness step; the reusable
2530	  algebraic core of \cref{lem:gr_existence_factor_through_valuation_ring} (it does not touch the
2531	  cofactor sub-step).}

2533	  \(f : R^I \to K\) along \(P^I_J\). For every size-\(d\) subset \(K'\),
2534	  \[
2535	    f'\bigl(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_{K'})\bigr)\cdot f(P^I_J) \;=\; f(P^I_{K'}),
2536	  \]

2543	  \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul}; \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away.lift\_eq}\) collapses the
2544	  two \(\mathrm{algebraMap}\)-images to \(f(P^I_J)\) and \(f(P^I_{K'})\), yielding the displayed
2545	  equation.
2546	\end{proof}

2643	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2644	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) satisfies the \emph{existence} part of the valuative
2645	  criterion: every commutative square from a valuation ring \(R\) (with fraction
2646	  field \(K\)) admits a diagonal filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).

2725	\begin{proof}
2726	  \uses{lem:gr_isProper_of_valuativeExistence, lem:gr_valuativeExistence_toSpecZ}
2727	  \leanok
2728	  Nitsure's argument is precisely the valuative criterion, organised here so that
2729	  all the topological/finiteness bookkeeping is separated from the one geometric
2730	  step. The reduction \cref{lem:gr_isProper_of_valuativeExistence} packages
2731	  quasi-compactness (\cref{lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ}), quasi-separatedness
2732	  (\cref{lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ}), local finiteness of type

2750	\section{Out of scope}
2751	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
2752	
2753	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
2754	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
2755	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
2756	are not treated here:
2757	\begin{itemize}
2758	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
2759	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
2760	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
2761	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
2762	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
2763	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
2764	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
2765	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
2766	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
2767	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
2768	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
2769	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
2770	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
2771	    that base change.
2772	\end{itemize}

1045	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
1046	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
1047	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
1048	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
1049	  \begin{itemize}

1051	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
1052	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
1053	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)

1056	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
1057	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),

1059	  \end{itemize}
1060	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
1061	  Hilbert function} is
1062	  \[
1063	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)

1068	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
1069	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
1070	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
1071	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},

1073	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
1074	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
1075	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).

1078	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
1079	
1080	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
1081	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
1082	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
1083	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
1084	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
1085	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).

1087	\begin{definition}\leanok
1088	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1089	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1090	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1091	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1092	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1093	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1094	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1095	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1096	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1097	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1098	\end{definition}

1295	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1296	
1297	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1298	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1299	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1300	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1301	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1302	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1303	
1304	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1305	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1306	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1308	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1309	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1310	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1311	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1312	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1313	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1314	\end{lemma}
1315	
1316	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1317	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1318	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1319	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1320	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1321	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1322	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1323	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1324	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1325	\end{lemma}
1326	
1327	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1328	[The kernel pair nests]
1329	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1330	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1331	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1332	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1333	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1334	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1335	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1336	\end{lemma}
1337	
1338	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1339	[The cokernel pair nests]
1340	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1342	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1343	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1344	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1345	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1346	\end{lemma}
1347	
1348	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1349	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1350	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1351	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1352	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1353	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1354	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1355	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1356	\end{lemma}
1357	
1358	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1359	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1360	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1362	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1363	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1364	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1365	\end{lemma}
1366	
1367	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1368	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1369	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1370	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1371	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1372	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1373	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1374	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1375	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1376	\end{lemma}
1377	
1378	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1379	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1380	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1381	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1382	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1383	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1384	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1385	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1386	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1387	\end{lemma}

1389	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1390	[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1391	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1392	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1393	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1394	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1395	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1396	  % length-(r-1) datum. The `\lean` pin names the `.ker` constructor — the single decl most
1397	  % representative of the bundled closure — so this dependency-bearing grouping node (which is
1398	  % `\uses`'d by the keystone rationality induction) stays wired into the graph rather than
1399	  % orphaning the component facts it `\uses`. This is a deliberate duplicate of the
1400	  % `def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker` pin.
1401	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1402	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1403	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1404	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1405	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1406	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1407	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1408	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1409	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1410	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1411	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1412	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1413	  \[
1414	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1415	    \qquad
1416	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1417	  \]
1418	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1419	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1420	  \begin{enumerate}
1421	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1422	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1423	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1424	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1425	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1426	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1427	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1428	  \end{enumerate}
1429	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1430	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1431	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1432	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1433	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1434	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1435	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1436	\end{lemma}
1437	
1438	\begin{proof}
1439	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1440	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1441	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1442	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1443	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1444	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1445	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1446	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1447	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1448	    \leanok
1449	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1450	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1451	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1452	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1453	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1454	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1455	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1456	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1457	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1458	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1459	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1460	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1461	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1462	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1463	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1464	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1465	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1466	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1467	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1468	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1469	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1470	
1471	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1472	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1473	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1474	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1475	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1476	
1477	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1478	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1479	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1480	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1481	
1482	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1483	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1484	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1485	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1486	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1487	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1488	\end{proof}

1490	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1491	
1492	\begin{lemma}
1493	
1494	\leanok
1495	[preimage dimension equals ambient-intersection dimension]
1496	  \label{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype}
1497	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finrank_comap_subtype}
1498	  For submodules \(p, S\) of a \(\kappa\)-vector space \(M\), the dimension of the
1499	  preimage of \(S\) under the inclusion \(p \hookrightarrow M\) equals the dimension of

500	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
501	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
502	
503	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
504	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
505	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
506	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
507	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
508	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
509	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
510	\end{lemma}
511	
512	\begin{proof}
513	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
514	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
515	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
516	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
517	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
518	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
519	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
520	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
521	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
522	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
523	
524	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
525	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
526	  \[
527	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
528	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
529	  \]
530	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
531	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
532	
533	  \medskip
534	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
535	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
536	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
537	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
538	
539	  \medskip
540	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
541	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
542	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
543	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
544	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
545	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
546	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
547	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
548	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
549	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
550	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
551	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
552	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
553	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
554	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
555	  an isomorphism.
556	
557	  \medskip
558	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
559	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
560	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
561	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
562	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
563	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
564	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
565	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
566	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
567	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
568	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
569	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
570	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
571	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
572	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
573	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
574	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
575	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
576	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
577	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
578	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
579	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
580	  movements.
581	
582	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
583	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
584	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
585	  \[
586	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
587	      \;\cong\;
588	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
589	  \]
590	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
591	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
592	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
593	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
594	
595	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
596	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
597	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
598	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of the
599	  family \(\{e_U\}_U\) (established in the proof of
600	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}), instantiated at the inclusion
601	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
602	  \[
603	    \begin{array}{ccc}
604	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
605	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
606	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
607	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
608	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
609	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
610	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
611	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)
612	    \end{array}
613	  \]
614	  commutes, using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
615	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
616	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
617	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
618	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
619	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
620	  \(\widetilde M\).
621	
622	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target restriction
623	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
624	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
625	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
626	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
627	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
628	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
629	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
630	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
631	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
632	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
633	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
634	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
635	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
636	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
637	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized
638	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
639	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
640	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
641	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
642	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
643	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
644	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
645	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
646	
647	  \medskip
648	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
649	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
650	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
651	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
652	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the naturality
653	  remark in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the family
654	  \(\{e_U\}_U\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the
655	  structure-sheaf restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this
656	  open-naturality that drives the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the
657	  per-\(a\) localization fact \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level
658	  localization Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with that
659	  naturality square for the inclusion
660	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square
661	  re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\). This is the single remaining
662	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
663	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
664	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
665	
666	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
667	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
668	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
669	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
670	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
671	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
672	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
673	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
674	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
675	
676	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
677	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
678	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
679	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
680	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
681	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
682	  % bump.
683	  \medskip
684	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
685	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
686	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
687	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
688	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
689	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
690	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
691	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
692	  needed for the affine lane.
693	\end{proof}
694	
695	\begin{remark}
696	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
697	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
698	  \begin{enumerate}
699	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
700	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
701	      \(R\)-module of sections of
702	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
703	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
704	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
705	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
706	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
707	      closed under isomorphism; hence
708	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
709	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
710	      needed for the affine lane.
711	  \end{enumerate}
712	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
713	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
714	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
715	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
716	\end{remark}

722	tilde-module. It is the affine case of the classical fact that the pullback of a
723	quasi-coherent sheaf is computed on modules by base change of scalars.

725	The pullback companion is built by uniqueness of left adjoints from the
726	per-object \(\Gamma\)-fragment comparison of

740	  \(\widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) on modules, exhibiting the
741	  pushforward dictionary as a natural transformation rather than merely a family of
