822	\subsection{The affine base-change lemma and its remaining obligations}
823	
824	The affine base-change lemma below proceeds \emph{directly on global sections}: it
825	reduces, via the locality criterion
826	Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
827	Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
828	to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from
829	the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
830	Mathlib. The first is the \emph{locality reduction}: restricting to the affine opens
831	of \(S'\) reduces the isomorphism claim to the affine--affine model
832	\(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\),
833	\(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\). The second is the
834	\emph{section-level identification}: in that affine--affine model, the global-sections
835	incarnation \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) of the base-change map, transported through the two
836	proved tilde dictionaries, is the canonical cancellation isomorphism
837	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) — an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis. This
838	is the honest cut of the affine close: locality on \(S'\), then a pure computation on
839	the module of global sections. No separate abstract adjoint-mate identification is
840	performed at the level of sheaves; the mate is unwound \emph{on sections}, where it
841	becomes \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) directly.
842	
843	\begin{lemma}
844	
845	\leanok
846	[Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map]
847	  \label{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}
848	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_map_affine_local}
849	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
850	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
851	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
852	  % "local on $S$ and $S'$" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
853	  % L920--926).
854	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
855	  % assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
856	  % $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
857	  % for some $A$-module $M$."
858	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
859	  change'' (proof, local-on-\(S\)-and-\(S'\) step).}
860	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
861	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of the
862	  base-change map (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible
863	  with restriction to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
864	  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
865	  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
866	  by the affine-open locality criterion
867	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
868	  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
869	  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
870	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
871	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
872	
873	\end{lemma}
874	
875	\begin{proof}
876	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
877	  By the affine-open locality criterion
878	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the base-change map is an isomorphism
879	  of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules as soon as its restriction to the sections over each
880	  affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) is an isomorphism. The only content beyond the bare criterion is to identify that
881	  restriction with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over
882	  \(U\). We obtain the identification by unfolding the definition of the map and
883	  pushing the restriction functor through each of its building blocks. The
884	  compatibility this needs is \emph{not} a new coherence theorem: it is the naturality
885	  of the adjunction transpose, together with the standard fact that pushforward
886	  commutes with restriction to an open of the base. We spell this out in three steps.
887	
888	  \medskip
889	  \emph{Step 1: unfold the map.} By
890	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, the base-change map is the image,
891	  under the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose — the natural bijection
892	  \(\operatorname{Hom}(g^*A, B) \cong \operatorname{Hom}(A, g_*B)\) — of the morphism
893	  \[
894	    f_*\mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\,f_*(\eta_{\mathcal{F}})\,}
895	    f_*(g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F} = g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F},
896	  \]
897	  where \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}} : \mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the unit of
898	  the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction and the identification
899	  \(f_*(g')_* = g_*\,f'_*\) is the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward applied to the
900	  square's commutativity \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\). Thus the map is, by
901	  construction, the transpose of the \(f_*\)-image of a unit — a composite of
902	  adjunction data and pushforward functors, with no ingredient beyond these.
903	
904	  \medskip
905	  \emph{Step 2: restriction to \(U\) commutes with each building block.} Fix an affine
906	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) and write \(\rho_U\) for restriction to \(U\) —
907	  equivalently, the section functor \(\Gamma(U, -)\) on \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules,
908	  or pullback along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow S'\). Restriction along
909	  \(U\) is a functor, and every ingredient of Step 1 is a natural transformation or a
910	  natural bijection; so applying \(\rho_U\) to the transpose-of-a-unit of Step 1
911	  returns the transpose-of-the-unit built from the restricted data. Concretely:
912	  \begin{itemize}
913	    \item[(a)] the unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}}\) of the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction
914	      restricts to the unit of the corresponding adjunction on the restricted square,
915	      because units are natural in their object and pulling the cartesian square back
916	      along \(U \hookrightarrow S'\) yields again a cartesian square (with total space
917	      the open \((f')^{-1}(U)\) of \(X'\));
918	    \item[(b)] \(f_*\) of a restricted morphism is the restriction of \(f_*\) of the
919	      morphism, because pushforward commutes with restriction to an open of the base:
920	      for an open \(U\) of the base one has
921	      \((f_*\mathcal{G})|_U = (f|_{f^{-1}U})_*\bigl(\mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}U}\bigr)\),
922	      naturally in \(\mathcal{G}\) — and likewise for \((g')_*\) and \(f'_*\);
923	    \item[(c)] the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose is a bijection natural in both
924	      variables, so it commutes with restricting its source and target along \(U\).
925	  \end{itemize}
926	  Chaining (a), (b), (c) — i.e.\ transporting the transpose-of-unit composite of
927	  Step 1 through the restriction functor \(\rho_U\) and replacing each block by its
928	  restricted counterpart — shows that the restriction of the base-change map to
929	  sections over \(U\) is itself the base-change map formed from the cartesian square
930	  restricted over \(U\). Choosing an affine open
931	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing the image \(g(U)\), affineness of
932	  \(f\) (local on the base and stable under base change) makes the total space
933	  restrict to an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and quasi-coherence of
934	  \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict to a tilde-module \(\widetilde{M}\) there; so this
935	  restricted map is exactly the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out
936	  over \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). The whole
937	  identification is thus definitional plus the naturalities (a)--(c); it introduces no
938	  obligation beyond them.
939	
940	  \medskip
941	  \emph{Step 3: conclude the reduction.} By Step 2 the per-affine-open hypothesis
942	  required by the locality criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} —
943	  that the base-change map restricts to an isomorphism on sections over each affine
944	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) — is, under the identification of Step 2, precisely the
945	  affine--affine section assertion. That assertion is supplied, with \emph{no} flatness
946	  hypothesis, by the section-level computation
947	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, which identifies the
948	  affine--affine base-change map on global sections with the cancellation isomorphism
949	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\). Applying the locality criterion
950	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} to this family of per-open
951	  isomorphisms shows that the base-change map is an isomorphism, as asserted.
952	\end{proof}

954	\subsection{The section-level base-change identity}
955	
956	The section-level identification
957	Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} below is the single
958	highest-leverage obligation of the affine close. In the affine--affine model the
959	base-change map \(\theta\) is, after its domain and codomain are read through the two
960	proved tilde dictionaries, a concrete \(R'\)-linear map
961	\(R' \otimes_R M \to (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\), and the content of the lemma is
962	that this map is the inverse of the cancellation isomorphism
963	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\). Because the base-change map is \emph{defined} as the
964	transpose of the inner pushforward comparison \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) under the
965	\((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-adjunction (\(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\)), its section-level value
966	is read off by the \emph{conjugate-counit calculus}: unfolding the transpose through the
967	counit form of the hom-set adjunction
968	(\(\operatorname{Adjunction.homEquiv\_counit}\)) factors \(\theta\) as
969	\(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by the geometric counit \(\varepsilon_g\), and
970	conjugating this composite by the two tilde dictionaries
971	\(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}) and
972	\(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) replaces the
973	geometric counit by the \emph{algebraic} extension-of-scalars counit along \(\psi\). This
974	is the route the live formalization takes: the cancellation identity
975	Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} routes through the generator
976	trace Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace}, which is a one-line corollary of the
977	section identity Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_section_identity}, whose single residual
978	obligation is the counit-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}.

980	opaque geometric composite on \(1 \otimes m\) produces no element-level normal form, so the
981	abstract conjugate calculus is the only tractable route. Two Mathlib results are recorded
982	first as dependency anchors.

1243	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1244	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1245	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,

1248	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1249	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1250	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1252	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1253	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1254	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1255	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1256	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1257	\end{lemma}
1258	\begin{proof}
1259	
1260	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1261	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1262	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1263	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1264	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1265	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1266	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1267	\end{proof}
1268	
1269	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1270	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1271	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1272	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1273	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1274	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1275	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1276	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1277	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1278	\end{lemma}
1279	\begin{proof}
1280	
1281	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1282	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1283	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1284	\end{proof}

1290	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1291	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1292	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1294	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1295	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1296	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1297	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1298	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1299	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1300	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1301	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1302	  % L933--938).
1303	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1304	  % equality
1305	  % $$
1306	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1307	  % $$
1308	  % as $R'$-modules."
1309	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1310	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1311	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1312	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1313	  \[
1314	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1315	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1316	  \]
1317	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1318	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1319	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1320	  hypothesis.
1321	\end{lemma}
1322	
1323	\begin{proof}
1324	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1325	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1326	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the natively \(R'\)-linear
1327	  pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1328	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1329	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1330	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1331	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1332	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1333	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1334	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1335	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1336	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1337	
1338	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1339	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1340	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1341	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1342	  dictionaries,
1343	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1344	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1345	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1346	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1347	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1348	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1349	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1350	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1351	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1352	  \[
1353	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1354	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1355	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1356	  \]
1357	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1358	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1359	
1360	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1361	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1362	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1363	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1364	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1365	\end{proof}

1446	  adjunctions between the same pair of categories, with units \(\eta_1\) and \(\eta_2\), and let
1447	  \(\alpha : L_2 \Rightarrow L_1\) be a natural transformation of the left adjoints. Write

1513	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1514	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1515	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1516	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1517	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1518	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1519	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype,
1520	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1521	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1522	  % NOTE: The proof factors the two kernel-dimension computations through the
1523	  %   helper \cref{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype} (preimage dimension = ambient
1524	  %   intersection dimension).
1525	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1526	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1527	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1528	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1529	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1530	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1531	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1532	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1533	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1534	  \[
1535	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1536	  \]
1537	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1538	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1539	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1540	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1541	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1542	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1543	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1544	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1545	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1546	\end{lemma}
1547	
1548	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1549	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1550	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1551	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1552	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1553	%  following short exact sequence
1554	%  $$
1555	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1556	%  $$
1557	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1558	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1559	\begin{proof}
1560	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1561	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1562	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1563	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1564	    \leanok
1565	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1566	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1567	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1568	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1569	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1570	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1571	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1572	
1573	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1574	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1575	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1576	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1577	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1578	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1579	
1580	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1581	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1582	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1583	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1584	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1585	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1586	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1587	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1588	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1589	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1590	
1591	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1592	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1593	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1594	  \[
1595	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1596	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1597	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1598	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1599	  \]
1600	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1601	\end{proof}

1603	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1604	
1605	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1606	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1607	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1608	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1609	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1610	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1611	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1612	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1613	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1614	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1615	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1616	
1617	\begin{definition}\leanok
1618	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1619	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1620	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1621	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1622	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1623	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1624	  ring homomorphism}
1625	  \[
1626	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1627	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1628	  \]
1629	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1630	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1631	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1632	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1633	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1634	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1635	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1636	\end{definition}
1637	
1638	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1639	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1640	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1641	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1642	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1643	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1644	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1645	  \(t_i\).
1646	\end{lemma}
1647	
1648	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1649	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]
1650	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1651	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_C}
1652	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1653	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\) for each \(c \in \kappa\):
1654	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and
1655	  carries the constant polynomial \(C\,c\) to the scalar endomorphism \(c \cdot 1\).
1656	\end{lemma}
1657	
1658	\begin{definition}\leanok
1659	[Polynomial-ring module structure on $M$]
1660	  \label{def:graded_polyModule}
1661	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule}
1662	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1663	  The \emph{polynomial-module structure} is the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module
1664	  structure on \(M\) obtained by restricting scalars along
1665	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}): a polynomial \(p\) acts on
1666	  \(m \in M\) by \(p \cdot m = \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m)\). This is the module over the
1667	  free polynomial ring on which the ambient subquotient induction's finiteness condition
1668	  is measured.
1669	\end{definition}
1670	
1671	\begin{lemma}\leanok

1677	  t_i(m)\) for all \(m \in M\). Immediate from

1680	
1681	\begin{lemma}\leanok

1692	\begin{lemma}\leanok

1705	\begin{definition}\leanok
1706	[Polynomial-ring submodule from a $t$-stable submodule]
1707	  \label{def:graded_polySubmodule}

1731	\subsubsection*{Finiteness-transfer infrastructure}
1732	
1733	The ambient finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer})
1734	factors the polynomial action of the larger ring through a surjection onto the
1735	smaller one. The following blocks build that surjection and the semilinearity
1736	identity it rests on, and record the two elementary finiteness manipulations
1737	(shrinking a numerator, enlarging a denominator) used to feed the kernel and
1738	cokernel constructors.
1739	
1740	\begin{definition}
1741	
1742	\leanok
1743	[The last-variable surjection of polynomial rings]
1744	  \label{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom}
1746	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_castSucc}
1747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_last}
1748	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_C}
1749	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_rename_castSucc}
1750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_surjective}
1751	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_ringHomSurjective}
1752	  \uses{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1753	  For a field \(\kappa\) and \(r \in \mathbb{N}\), the \emph{last-variable
1754	  homomorphism} is the \(\kappa\)-algebra map
1755	  \[
1756	    \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1757	      = \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r\}, \kappa)
1758	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1759	      \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r-1\}, \kappa) = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1760	  \]
1761	  obtained by evaluation: it sends the last variable \(X_r\) to \(0\) and each
1762	  earlier variable \(X_i\) (for \(0 \le i < r\)) to \(X_i\), and fixes constants.
1763	  It is a left inverse of the algebra map
1764	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1765	  induced by the canonical inclusion \(\{0, \dots, r-1\} \hookrightarrow \{0, \dots, r\}\)
1766	  on variable indices, and is therefore surjective. This is the concrete
1767	  ring surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1768	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) along which finiteness is transferred
1769	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
1770	\end{definition}
1771

1900	  Write \(\sigma = \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1901	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the surjective ring
1902	  homomorphism sending the last variable to \(0\) and each earlier variable \(X_i\)
1903	  (\(i < r\)) to \(X_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}). Consider the map on numerators
1904	  \[
1905	    g : \mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P \;\longrightarrow\;
1906	      \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/
1907	        \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr), \qquad y \longmapsto [\,y\,],
1908	  \]
1909	  where the source carries the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-module structure and the
1910	  target the reduced \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module structure (both with the
1911	  same ambient carrier \(P\), \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). The mod-\(P'\)
1912	  semilinearity identity (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}) -- acting by
1913	  \(s\) through the full family and acting by \(\sigma(s)\) through the reduced family
1914	  agree modulo \(P'\), precisely because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\) --
1915	  says exactly that \(g\) is \(\sigma\)-semilinear. The map \(g\) is surjective (it is
1916	  the quotient projection on the common carrier), and it kills the denominator
1917	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\), so it descends through that submodule
1918	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}) to a surjective
1919	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear map
1920	  \[

1922	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\;
1923	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1924	  \]
1925	  from the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finite subquotient onto the reduced one.
1926	  Finiteness transfers along this surjection
1927	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}), giving finiteness of \(P/P'\)
1928	  over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\).
1929	
1930	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1931	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), not the subalgebra
1932	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq
1933	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1934	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection
1935	  \(\sigma\) that the scalar transfer requires
1936	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}). Keeping free polynomial
1937	  rings at every step keeps \(\sigma\) -- and hence the transfer -- available
1938	  throughout. Everything is finite generation of ambient submodules of the fixed
1939	  \(M\) and their quotients.

1664	\leanok
1665	[Pseudofunctoriality of the pullback--pushforward unit]
1666	  \label{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1667	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1668	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1669	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1670	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to X_3\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
1671	  \(N\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_3}\)-module. Write \(\eta^{h}\) for the unit of the
1672	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \((h^{*}\dashv h_{*})\) attached to a morphism \(h\), and
1673	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\), \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b}\) for the pseudofunctor
1674	  composition coherences. Then
1675	  \[
1676	    \eta^{b}_{N} \;\circ\; b_{*}\bigl(\eta^{a}_{\,b^{*}N}\bigr)
1677	      \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{N}
1678	    \;=\;
1679	    \eta^{a\circ b}_{N} \;\circ\;
1680	      (a\circ b)_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_{N}\bigr),
1681	  \]
1682	  where composites are read left to right. In words: the unit of the composite morphism
1683	  \(a \circ b\), reindexed by the inverse pullback coherence, factors as the \(b\)-unit, followed
1684	  by \(b_{*}\) of the \(a\)-unit, followed by the pushforward coherence. This is the leg-reindex

1686	  isomorphism and \(b\) an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map it turns the generic projection-leg
1687	  unit into the affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-unit modulo the transparent coherences.

1689	\begin{proof}
1690	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1691	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1692	  Apply the unit-across-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib} to the
1693	  composite adjunction \((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}) . (a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\), the adjunction
1694	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1695	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1696	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by
1697	  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} that mate is exactly the pushforward
1698	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}\). Expanding the unit of the composite
1699	  adjunction by Lemma~\ref{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib} —

1702	  the stated equation.

1756	[$\Gamma$-collapse of the pushforward-congruence coherence]

1777	  \(\operatorname{eqToHom}\) transport along the induced equality of section modules. It carries no

2045	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
2046	
2047	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2048	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
2049	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
2050	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
2051	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2052	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2053	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
2054	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2055	  \[
2056	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2057	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2058	  \]
2059	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2060	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2061	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2062	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2063	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2064	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2065	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2066	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2067	  \[
2068	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2069	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2070	  \]
2071	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2072	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2073	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2074	\end{lemma}
2075	
2076	\begin{proof}
2077	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2078	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2079	    \leanok
2080	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2081	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2082	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2083	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2084	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2085	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2086	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2087	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2088	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2089	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2090	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2091	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
2092	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
2093	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
2094	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2095	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
2096	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
2097	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
2098	\end{proof}

2102	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2103	[Base-case finiteness over the empty polynomial ring]
2104	  \label{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2106	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over
2107	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\) for an \emph{empty} index set
2108	  \(\sigma\) -- in particular \(\sigma = \{0, \dots, r-1\}\) with \(r = 0\) -- in a way
2109	  compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action (scalar tower). If \(Q\) is module-finite over
2110	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\), then \(Q\) is finite-dimensional over
2111	  \(\kappa\). Indeed for empty \(\sigma\) the polynomial ring is
2112	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
2113	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
2114	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
2115	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a
2116	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space, hence \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero.

2119	\begin{lemma}
2120	
2121	\leanok
2122	[independence of images modulo the kernel]
2123	  \label{lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2124	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2125	  Let \(\pi : A \to Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map and \((g_i)_{i \in \iota}\) a family of
2126	  subspaces of \(A\) that is \emph{independent modulo \(\ker\pi\)}: for each \(i\), every
2127	  \(a \in g_i\) that also lies in \(\ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\) already lies in
2128	  \(\ker\pi\). Then the image family \((\pi(g_i))_{i \in \iota}\) is independent
2129	  (\(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\)) in \(Q\). Ring-agnostic lattice core of the base-case
2130	  independence step below; applied to the quotient map \(N \twoheadrightarrow N/N'\).
2131	\end{lemma}
2132	\begin{proof}
2133	
2134	\leanok
2135	  By the disjointness criterion for independence it suffices to show, for each \(i\),
2136	  that \(\pi(g_i) \cap \sum_{j \ne i} \pi(g_j) = 0\). Take \(q\) in that intersection;
2137	  write \(q = \pi(a)\) with \(a \in g_i\), and (pushing the sum through \(\pi\), since
2138	  \(\pi(\sum_{j\ne i} g_j) = \sum_{j\ne i}\pi(g_j)\)) \(q = \pi(b)\) with
2139	  \(b \in \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). Then \(a - b \in \ker\pi\), so
2140	  \(a = (a-b) + b \in \ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). By the hypothesis applied at \(i\),
2141	  \(a \in \ker\pi\), whence \(q = \pi(a) = 0\).
2142	\end{proof}

2146	\leanok

2185	  degree \(n\), composing the inclusion of the homogeneous piece

2187	  \(N \twoheadrightarrow Q_0\) gives a \(\kappa\)-linear map
2188	  \(\psi_n : N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \to Q_0\), \(m \mapsto [m]\), whose image is the
2189	  class of the degree-\(n\) homogeneous piece inside the subquotient.
2190	
2191	  \emph{Step 1: independence of the images.} The family
2192	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\bigr)_{n}\) is \(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\) in
2193	  \(Q_0\). The reason is the ambient direct-sum grading of \(M\): the homogeneous
2194	  pieces \(\mathcal{M}_n\) form an independent family
2195	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}). An element of
2196	  \(\bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)\) is a finite sum of classes of
2197	  homogeneous elements of degrees \(j \ne n\); since \(N'\) is homogeneous, the
2198	  degree-\(n\) homogeneous component of any representative of such a class lies in
2199	  \(N'\), so the class has zero degree-\(n\) component in \(Q_0\). A nonzero class in
2200	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\), by contrast, is represented by a homogeneous
2201	  element of degree \(n\) not in \(N'\), so has nonzero degree-\(n\) component.
2202	  Comparing degree-\(n\) components forces the intersection
2203	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \cap \bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)
2204	  = 0\); independence follows.
2205	  % NOTE: ROUTE (b). Prove the iSupIndep by direct membership analysis in
2206	  %   ⨆_{j ≠ n} range(ψ j), reading off the degree-n homogeneous component
2207	  %   directly inside Q₀'s fixed κ-module structure.
2208	  %   No outgoing linear map out of Q₀ is constructed, so no scalar ring other than κ
2209	  %   is ever introduced.
2210	  % NOTE: ROUTE (a). Building a κ-linear detector
2211	  %   Φ : Q₀ → M/N', [m] ↦ [π_n m] (degree-n projection) via descent through the
2212	  %   subquotient fails: the descended map carries the scalar action of Q₀'s base
2213	  %   ring, but the target M/N' carries only a κ-module structure -- a scalar-ring
2214	  %   incompatibility. Route (b) avoids this by working entirely within Q₀.
2215	
2216	  \emph{Step 2: finiteness of the support.} Because \(Q_0\) is finite-dimensional and
2217	  the ranges \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\) are independent, only finitely many of
2218	  them are nonzero: the set \(\{\,n : \operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \ne 0\,\}\) is
2219	  finite (\cref{lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib}). In particular it is bounded
2220	  above, by some \(K \in \mathbb{N}\).
2221	
2222	  \emph{Step 3: eventual vanishing.} For \(n > K\) we have
2223	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) = 0\), i.e.\ every homogeneous element of
2224	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\) has class \(0\) in \(Q_0 = N/N'\); equivalently
2225	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N'\), hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)
2226	  and
2227	  \[
2228	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2229	      - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n) \;=\; 0 .
2230	  \]
2231	  Thus \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero, and
2232	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2233	\end{proof}

2284	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} A length-zero datum has a finiteness witness making the
2285	  subquotient \(N/N'\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space
2286	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}); its degreewise
2287	  images form an independent family inside that finite-dimensional space, so only
2288	  finitely many are nonzero and \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for \(n \gg 0\)

2290	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is rational of order
2291	  \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).

2300	  induction hypothesis applied

2302	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise

2304	  \[

2311	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
2312	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.

2315	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2316	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2317	
2318	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2319	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2320	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2321	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2322	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2323

2326	\leanok
2327	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2328	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2329	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2330	  % NOTE: The definition carries no \uses: the Lean construction is

2337	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2338	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies

2341	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2342	  % Polynomials" (read from
2343	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2344	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2345	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2346	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2347	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2348	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2349	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2350	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2351	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2352	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2353	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2354	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2355	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2356	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2357	  \[
2358	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2359	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2360	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2361	  \]
2362	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2363	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2364	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2365	
2366	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2367	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2368	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2369	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2370	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2371	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2372	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2373	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2374	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2375	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2376	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2377	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2378	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2379	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2380	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2381	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2382	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2383	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2384	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2385	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2386	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2387	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2388	
2389	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2390	\end{definition}

1826	  to the identity, so the right-hand side collapses to \(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\), giving the stated
1827	  expansion.

1936	\begin{lemma}[Conjugation distributes over composition (reassoc simp lemma)]
1937	  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib}
1938	  \lean{CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv_comp}
1939	  \mathlibok
1940	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1941	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\), \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\),
1942	  \(\mathrm{adj}_3 : L_3 \dashv R_3\) be adjunctions between the same pair of categories, and let
1943	  \(\alpha : L_2 \Rightarrow L_1\), \(\beta : L_3 \Rightarrow L_2\) be transformations of left adjoints.
1944	  Then the conjugates compose, with order reversed onto the right-adjoint side:
1945	  \[
1946	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_2)\,\alpha \;\circ\;
1947	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_2,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,\beta
1948	      \;=\; \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,(\beta \circ \alpha) .
1949	  \]
1950	  Tagged \(\mathtt{@[reassoc (attr := simp)]}\), it is the lead member of the conjugate
1951	  reassociation simp set: it fuses the per-adjunction conjugates of a composite adjunction into the
1952	  conjugate of the composite (the conjugate-side form of the paste-of-mates).
1953	\end{lemma}
1954

1956	  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_symm_comp_mathlib}
1957	  \lean{CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv_symm_comp}
1958	  \mathlibok
1959	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1960	  The inverse-direction companion of Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib}: for transformations
1961	  \(\alpha : R_1 \Rightarrow R_2\), \(\beta : R_2 \Rightarrow R_3\) of the right adjoints,
1962	  \[
1963	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_2,\mathrm{adj}_3)^{-1}\beta \;\circ\;
1964	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_2)^{-1}\alpha
1965	      \;=\; \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_3)^{-1}(\alpha \circ \beta) ,
1966	  \]
1967	  also \(\mathtt{@[reassoc (attr := simp)]}\). Together these two close the reassociation bookkeeping of
1968	  the conjugate-side discharge.
1969	\end{lemma}

2500	  \]
2501	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2502	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2503	
2504	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2505	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2506	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2507	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2508	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2509	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2510	\end{lemma}
2511	
2512	\begin{proof}
2513	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2514	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2515	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2516	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2517	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2518	
2519	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2520	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2521	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2522	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2523	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2524	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2525	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2526	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2527	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2528	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2529	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2530	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2531	\end{proof}

2572	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2573	  \begin{enumerate}
2574	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2575	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2576	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2577	    \item the section-restriction map
2578	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2579	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2580	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2581	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2582	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2583	  \end{enumerate}
2584	\end{lemma}
2585	
2586	\begin{proof}
2587	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib, lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization,
2588	    lem:qcoh_pullback_fromSpec, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
2589	    lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general, lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence,
2590	    lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear, def:gamma_image_ring_equiv,
2591	    lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_hP1}
2592	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2593	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2594	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2595	
2596	  Part (2) is the \emph{gap-2 transport}. Because \(U\) is affine, Mathlib's
2597	  canonical affine immersion \(\mathrm{fromSpec} : \Spec \Gamma(X, U) \to X\) has
2598	  image \(U\) and identifies \(U\) with the spectrum of its ring of sections. We
2599	  obtain (2) by instantiating the general-ambient-scheme transport core
2600	  \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general} at the affine open immersion
2601	  \(j := \mathrm{fromSpec}\), then bridging its conclusion to the basic-open
2602	  restriction.
2603	
2604	  \emph{Supplying the P1 datum.} The core requires
2605	  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,
2606	  \mathcal{M}).\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\bigr)\). This is produced in two steps: first the
2607	  pullback \((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal{M}\) is
2608	  again quasi-coherent by \cref{lem:qcoh_pullback_fromSpec} (quasi-coherence is
2609	  preserved under pullback along \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\)); then, since this pullback
2610	  lives on the affine scheme \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\),
2611	  \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ} turns that quasi-coherence into the
2612	  required isomorphism of the \(\widetilde{(-)}\)-counit.
2613	
2614	  \emph{Applying the core.} Feeding this P1 datum, the element \(f\), and the slice
2615	  element \(f' := \sigma^{-1}(f)\) --- where
2616	  \(\sigma = (\Gamma\Spec\text{-iso})^{-1} \circ
2617	  \mathrm{gammaImageRingEquiv}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}\,\top\)
2618	  (\cref{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}) is the \(\top\)-level section ring iso, so the
2619	  transport hypothesis \(\sigma(f') = f\) holds by construction --- to
2620	  \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general} yields that the section
2621	  restriction
2622	  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top) \to
2623	  \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,D(f'))\) is
2624	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(\Gamma(X, U)\).
2625	
2626	  \emph{Bridging to the basic-open restriction.} It remains to identify this
2627	  statement, posed on the image opens \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top\) and
2628	  \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,D(f')\), with
2629	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) for the consumer-facing
2630	  basic-open restriction \(\mathrm{restrictBasicOpenₗ}\)
2631	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear}). The image opens are
2632	  \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top = U\) and
2633	  \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,D(f') = D(f)\). The one nontrivial coherence is that,
2634	  under the identification \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top = U\), the section ring
2635	  iso \(\sigma\) restricts to the identity --- precisely \(\rho(\sigma\,f') = f\),
2636	  where \(\rho\) is the presheaf transport along that open identification --- which
2637	  is exactly the section-coherence crux
2638	  \cref{lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence}. With it the localized-module
2639	  property transports across the open identification isomorphisms to the
2640	  basic-open restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\), establishing (2).
2641	\end{proof}

2644	
2645	\leanok
2646	[Basic-open localization given the pullback-$\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ isomorphism datum]
2647	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_hP1}
2648	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_hP1}
2649	  \uses{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general, lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence,
2650	    lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear, lem:isLocalizedModule_ringEquiv_semilinear,
2651	    def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}
2652	  \textit{The $\mathrm{hP1}$-explicit form of \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} part (2):
2653	  the basic-open restriction localizes when the quasi-coherence of the pullback is given as a hypothesis,
2654	  and the proof reduces to a purely mechanical open/ring-iso bridge.}
2655	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\), \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open with
2656	  canonical affine immersion \(\mathrm{fromSpec} : \Spec \Gamma(X,U) \to X\), and \(f \in \Gamma(X,U)\).
2657	  Suppose
2658	  \(\mathrm{hP1} : \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,
2659	  \mathcal{M}).\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\bigr)\). Then the basic-open restriction
2660	  \(\mathrm{restrictBasicOpenₗ}\,\mathcal{M}\,f : \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, U) \to
2661	  \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f)\) is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over
2662	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\).

2664	\begin{proof}
2665	  \uses{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general, lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence,
2666	    lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear, lem:isLocalizedModule_ringEquiv_semilinear,
2667	    def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}
2668	  Instantiate the general-ambient transport core \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general} at the
2669	  affine open immersion \(j := \mathrm{fromSpec}\) (its base ring is then \(\Gamma(X,U)\)), with slice
2670	  element \(f' := \sigma^{-1}(f)\) where \(\sigma = \mathrm{gammaImageRingEquiv}\,j\,\top\)
2671	  (\cref{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}); the transport hypothesis \(\sigma(f') = f\) holds by construction.
2672	  Fed the datum \(\mathrm{hP1}\), the core yields \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f')\) for
2673	  the restriction \(\mathrm{restrictₗ}\,\mathcal{M} : \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, j\,''^{U}\top) \to
2674	  \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, j\,''^{U}D(f'))\) over \(A := \Gamma(X, j\,''^{U}\top)\). Transport this to the
2675	  consumer-facing basic-open restriction across: (i) the open identifications \(j\,''^{U}\top = U\) and
2676	  \(j\,''^{U}D(f') = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) (the latter Mathlib's
2677	  \(\mathrm{IsAffineOpen.fromSpec\_image\_basicOpen}\)); (ii) the section ring iso \(\rho : A \cong
2678	  \Gamma(X,U)\) induced by \(j\,''^{U}\top = U\), for which \(\rho(\sigma f') = f\) is the section-coherence
2679	  crux \cref{lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence}; and (iii) the semilinear localization-transfer
2680	  lemma \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_ringEquiv_semilinear} along \(\rho\), with
2681	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,f').\mathrm{map}\,\rho = \mathrm{powers}\,f\) by \(\mathrm{Submonoid.map\_powers}\).
2682	  This yields \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) for
2683	  \(\mathrm{restrictBasicOpenₗ}\,\mathcal{M}\,f\) (\cref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear}).
2684	\end{proof}

2702	\end{proof}

2725	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2726	[Section coherence of the affine immersion $\mathrm{fromSpec}$]
2727	  \label{lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence}
2728	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence}
2729	  \uses{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}
2730	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, with canonical affine immersion
2731	  \(\mathrm{fromSpec} : \Spec \Gamma(X, U) \to X\) of image \(U\), and write
2732	  \(e_{\top} : \mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top = U\) for the canonical identification of its image
2733	  with \(U\). Let
2734	  \(\sigma = (\Gamma\Spec\text{-iso})^{-1} \circ \mathrm{gammaImageRingEquiv}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}\,\top\)
2735	  (\cref{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}) be the \(\top\)-level section ring isomorphism, and let
2736	  \(\rho : \mathcal{O}_X(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X(\mathrm{fromSpec}''^{U}\,\top)\) be the
2737	  structure-sheaf isomorphism induced by \(e_{\top}\). Then \(\rho \circ \sigma = \mathrm{id}\), i.e.,
2738	  \(\rho(\sigma\,f) = f\) for every \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\).
2739	\end{lemma}
2740	\begin{proof}
2741	  The composite \(\rho \circ \sigma\) is a ring endomorphism of \(\Gamma(X, U)\). Expanding the
2742	  definition of \(\sigma\) via \cref{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv} and applying the self-application
2743	  identity of \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\) over \(U\)---which identifies the image of the total open of
2744	  \(\Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) under \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\) with \(U\)---together with presheaf naturality,
2745	  the composite \(\rho \circ \sigma\) reduces to a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra endomorphism of
2746	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\). Since the only such endomorphism is the identity, \(\rho(\sigma\,f) = f\).
2747	\end{proof}

2775	  localizes the slice restriction \(\Gamma(M', \top) \to \Gamma(M', D(f'))\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f'\)

2777	  \(e_1 = \mathrm{gammaPullbackImageIso}\,j\,\mathcal{M}\,\top\) and

2889	  \(W_i := g^{-1}(q.X_i)\). Then the geometric restriction
2890	  \((W_i.\iota^{*})\,N\) admits a \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.Presentation}\) on \(W_i.\mathrm{toScheme}\).

2894	    lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}

2896	  \(k : W_i.\mathrm{toScheme} \to (q.X_i).\mathrm{toScheme}\) on preimages, characterized by the
2897	  commuting square \(k \circ (q.X_i).\iota = W_i.\iota \circ g\) (the morphism induced by \(g\) between
2898	  the inclusion of \(W_i = g^{-1}(q.X_i)\) and that of \(q.X_i\)). By pseudofunctoriality of pullback
2899	  (\(\mathrm{pullbackComp}\), named in prose) this square gives a natural isomorphism
2900	  \[
2901	    (W_i.\iota^{*})\,N \;=\; (W_i.\iota^{*})\,(g^{*}M)
2902	      \;\cong\; (W_i.\iota \circ g)^{*}M
2903	      \;=\; (k \circ (q.X_i).\iota)^{*}M
2904	      \;\cong\; k^{*}\bigl((q.X_i).\iota^{*}\,M\bigr) .
2905	  \]
2906	  Now \((q.X_i).\iota^{*}\,M\) carries the geometric presentation
2907	  \cref{def:presentation_pullback_iota_of_quasicoherentData}. Apply \(\mathrm{Presentation.map}\)
2908	  (\cref{lem:presentation_map_mathlib}) along the pullback functor \(k^{*}\)
2909	  (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}), which preserves colimits and carries the unit sheaf to the
2910	  unit sheaf, to obtain a presentation of \(k^{*}\bigl((q.X_i).\iota^{*}\,M\bigr)\); transport it across
2911	  the displayed isomorphism via \(\mathrm{Presentation.ofIsIso}\) to land a presentation of
2912	  \((W_i.\iota^{*})\,N\).

3004	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, item (4); Spec-local core of
3005	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}, part (2).}
3006	  Let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module and \(f \in R\). Then the presheaf restriction map of the
3007	  associated sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\) on \(\Spec R\) from the global sections
3008	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, \top)\) to the sections on the basic open \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\)
3009	  exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\) as the localized module
3010	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\); that is, it is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\)
3011	  over \(R\).
3012	\end{lemma}
3013	\begin{proof}
3014	  Mathlib supplies the localization-out-of-\(N\) instance
3015	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) for the canonical map
3016	  \(N \to \Gamma(\widetilde N, D(f))\). Since the global-sections map
3017	  \(N \to \Gamma(\widetilde N, \top)\) is an isomorphism and the canonical map to \(D(f)\) factors
3018	  through it followed by the restriction, precomposing the localization map with the inverse
3019	  isomorphism transfers the \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\) property to the restriction map
3020	  (Mathlib's transfer lemma for localization along a linear equivalence).
3021	\end{proof}

3023	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3024	[Basic-open restriction localizes for a sheaf in the essential image of $\widetilde{(-)}$]
3025	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}

3027	  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}

3029	  restriction localizes for any module sheaf that is \(\widetilde{N}\) up to isomorphism.}
3030	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) such that the counit
3031	  \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism (equivalently, \(M\) lies in the essential image of
3032	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\); Mathlib's \(\mathrm{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_iff}\)), and let \(f \in R\). Then the
3033	  presheaf restriction of \(M\) from \(\Gamma(M, \top)\) to \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\) is

3038	  forgetting to the presheaf gives a natural isomorphism \(\psi\) whose components are isomorphisms
3039	  of \(R\)-modules. Its naturality square for the inclusion \(D(f) \hookrightarrow \top\)
3040	  intertwines the restriction maps of \(\widetilde{N}\) (for \(N = \Gamma(M, \top)\)) and of \(M\).
3041	  Transporting \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict} across the two component isomorphisms
3042	  (pre- and post-composing by the linear equivalences induced by the components of \(\psi\))
3043	  identifies the restriction of \(M\) with a localization map, hence it is
3044	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\).

3052	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) and \(f \in R\). Then \(f\) acts as a unit

3060	  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}, D(f)) = R_f\) being the away-localization
3061	  (\(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\)), through which the \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\)
3062	  factors.

3339	  \label{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}
3340	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}
3341	  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib, lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}
3342	  \textit{The topological finite-cover front of the gap1 transport: any quasi-coherence cover refines
3343	  to a finite basic-open cover.}
3344	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) and let \(q\) be quasi-coherence data for \(M\)
3345	  (\cref{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}): a — possibly infinite — open cover
3346	  \(\{q.X_i\}\) of \(\top\) together with a presentation of \(M.\mathrm{over}\,(q.X_i)\) on each
3347	  member. Then there is a \emph{finite} subset \(t \subseteq R\) whose basic opens cover \(\Spec R\)
3348	  (equivalently \(\mathrm{span}\,t = (1)\)) such that each \(D(r)\), \(r \in t\), is contained in some
3349	  member \(q.X_i\) of the presentation cover.
3350	\end{lemma}
3351	\begin{proof}
3352	  \uses{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}
3353	  The set \(G = \{\,r \in R : D(r) \le q.X_i \text{ for some } i\,\}\) spans the unit ideal. Indeed
3354	  every prime of \(R\) lies in some member \(q.X_i\) by the covering property \(q.\mathrm{coversTop}\)
3355	  (read off the \(\mathrm{Opens}\) Grothendieck-topology sieve), and the basic opens form a basis of
3356	  the Zariski topology (\cref{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}), so a basic open \(D(r) \subseteq
3357	  q.X_i\) is a neighbourhood of that prime; hence \(\bigsqcup_{r \in G} D(r) = \top\), which is to say
3358	  \(\mathrm{span}\,G = (1)\). A unit-ideal membership uses only finitely many of the generators,
3359	  yielding a finite \(t \subseteq G\) with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = (1)\) and each \(D(r) \le q.X_i\) for
3360	  \(r \in t\).
3361	\end{proof}

3411	[The over-equivalence functor is cocontinuous]
3412	  \label{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous}
3413	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous}
3414	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
3415	  \textit{Topological layer of bridge C; fills the \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} TODO.}
3416	  The functor of \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib})
3417	  is cocontinuous (cover-lifting) from the \(U\)-slice \(J_X|_U\) of the opens Grothendieck
3418	  topology of \(X\) to the opens Grothendieck topology \(J_U\) of the subspace \(U\).
3419	\end{lemma}
3420	\begin{proof}
3421	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
3422	  A sieve covers in the slice topology \(J_X|_U\) iff its image sieve covers in \(J_X\), and
3423	  covering for the opens topology is pointwise: a sieve covers iff its members' opens cover the
3424	  base set-theoretically. Given a point \(x\) of a slice object and a cover member in \(J_U\)
3425	  containing the image of \(x\), transport it across the open embedding \(U \hookrightarrow X\),
3426	  which is injective: the set-theoretic image of an open \(V \subseteq U\) is an open of \(X\)
3427	  contained in the base, a neighbourhood of \(x\) lying in the image sieve. This witnesses the
3428	  cover-lifting condition.
3429	\end{proof}

2461	  the \(\Gamma\)-pushforward dictionaries (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso},
2462	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}), the two pushforward-composition factors and the
2463	  congruence factor are transparent. The composition coherences collapse to the identity —
2464	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) and
2465	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f',g})^{\mathrm{inv}}\) evaluate, on the top open, to the identity
2466	  map on restriction of scalars (their section-level values are the identity) — and they
2467	  serve only to rebracket the iterated pushforward along the two factorizations
2468	  \((g' \circ f)_* = (f' \circ g)_*\) of the square's composite. (The
2469	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) collapse is a transparent re-association
2470	  that is most naturally accounted for together with the leg-reindex of step~(iii) rather than at

2472	  to a canonical transport along the equality of opens: its section-level value
2473	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w)^{\mathrm{hom}}\) is the
2474	  presheaf-restriction along the canonical equality of opens, i.e.\ a repackaging along
2475	  that equality carrying no substantive content. Thus after the \(\Gamma\)-collapse the only
2476	  surviving non-trivial factor is \(f_{*}\bigl(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\bigr)\) read on global
2477	  sections.

2480	  (step~(i)) to \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), the leg-reindex engine

2490	  the algebraic unit \(\eta_M : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of Seam~1
2491	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}). Composing the surviving factor with the
2492	  codomain identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)
2493	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) and reading over \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) by
2494	  restriction of scalars along \(\psi\) identifies all four factors with the restriction of
2495	  scalars along \(\psi\) of the codomain-read transport of the unit value, namely

3513	\leanok
3514	[The slice-to-geometric module-category equivalence (gap1, C, step 3)]
3515	  \label{def:over_restrict_equiv}

3517	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,

3519	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}
3520	  \textit{Module-level lift of the topological bridge C; the step-3 equivalence on which the
3521	  slice-touching isomorphism is built.}
3522	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open. The category of sheaves of modules over the
3523	  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) on the slice site \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U\) is
3524	  equivalent to the category \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{Modules}\) of sheaves of modules on the open
3525	  subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\):
3526	  \[
3527	    \mathrm{SheafOfModules}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\bigr)
3528	      \;\simeq\; U.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{Modules}.
3529	  \]
3530	  This is the module-category lift of the topological sheaf equivalence
3531	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}.
3532	\end{definition}
3533	\begin{proof}
3534	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,
3535	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous,
3536	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}
3537	  Apply the module-sheaf transport \(\mathrm{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\)
3538	  (\cref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}) to the site equivalence
3539	  \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}), whose two legs are
3540	  both continuous (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous},
3541	  \cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}). The transport consumes a pair of mutually inverse
3542	  ring-sheaf comparison morphisms between the sliced structure sheaf
3543	  \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) and the geometric structure sheaf
3544	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U.\mathrm{toScheme}}\); for these one takes the whiskered (co)unit of the site
3545	  equivalence \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) into the structure presheaf, paired with the
3546	  identity. The identity is legal precisely because the geometric structure sheaf is
3547	  the transport of the sliced one across the ring-valued instance of
3548	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr} (this is step~2 of the bridge, which holds by
3549	  construction). Orienting the resulting equivalence so that its functor runs from the slice
3550	  side to the geometric side gives the stated equivalence.
3551	\end{proof}

4343	  Let \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes and let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules
4344	  on \(X\). The global sections of the pullback module along \(j\) are canonically isomorphic to the
4345	  sections of \(M\) over the image of \(j\):

4353	  between them.

4362	  \(\Gamma(M, j(\top)) = \Gamma(M, \mathrm{range}\,j)\). The restriction functor is naturally

4439	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4440	[Ring-iso-semilinear localized-module transport (gap1 ingredient I)]
4441	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_ringEquiv_semilinear}
4442	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_of_ringEquiv_semilinear}
4443	  Let \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\) be a ring isomorphism, \(S \le R\) a submonoid, and
4444	  \(g : M_1 \to M_2\) an \(R\)-linear map satisfying \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\ S\ g\). Suppose
4445	  \(e_1 : M_1 \simeq_+ N_1\) and \(e_2 : M_2 \simeq_+ N_2\) are additive isomorphisms that are
4446	  \(\sigma\)-semilinear (\(e_i(a \cdot x) = \sigma(a) \cdot e_i(x)\)), and \(h : N_1 \to N_2\) is an
4447	  \(R'\)-linear map intertwining them, \(h(e_1 x) = e_2(g x)\). Then \(h\) satisfies
4448	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\ (S.\mathrm{map}\ \sigma)\ h\) over \(R'\).
4449	\end{lemma}
4450	\begin{proof}
4451	  Transport the three \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\) fields of \(g\) along the semilinear pair
4452	  \((e_1,e_2)\). \emph{map\_units:} the action of \(s' = \sigma(s)\) on \(N_2\) is conjugate via \(e_2\) to
4453	  the (bijective, since a unit) action of \(s\) on \(M_2\), hence bijective, hence a unit
4454	  (\(\mathrm{Module.End.isUnit\_iff}\)). \emph{surj:} given \(y \in N_2\), pull back \(e_2^{-1}y\), apply
4455	  the surjectivity field of \(g\) to get \(g\,m = s\cdot e_2^{-1}y\), and push forward by \(e_2\); the
4456	  image-submonoid witness is \(\langle \sigma s, s, s\in S, \mathrm{rfl}\rangle\). \emph{exists\_of\_eq:}
4457	  symmetric, pulling the equality back through \(e_1\). Mathlib provides only the same-ring
4458	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule.of\_linearEquiv}\); crossing a ring iso is the genuine gap.
4459	\end{proof}
